title: Acute Headache Management
text: ACUTE HEADACHE HISTORY:  New headache or history of headaches  Location, quality, severity  Onset (sudden or gradual)  Aura  Transient visual obscurations  Associated features (nausea/vomiting/photophobia/phonophobia/ diplopia/tinnitus)  What medications and how much have they taken for the headache  Worsening factors (e.g. lying down, standing up, straining)  Diurnal pattern (e.g. worse in the morning)  OCP or Vitamin A/retinoid use Chronic headache syndrome: ask about family history of headaches and screen for lifestyle factors: caffeine use, sleep, hydration status, frequency of analgesic use, relation to menses, stress, relation to weather New headache syndrome: screen for red flags such as recent head or neck trauma, chiropractor visits, fevers or chills, recent travel or sick contacts, altered mental state, neck stiffness, focal neurologic or visual deficits New headache in the elderly: screen for GCA symptoms such as fever, weight loss, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, proximal limb myalgias, constitutional symptoms EXAM: Vital signs with particular attention to blood pressure and temperature, neurologic exam including a fundoscopic and visual fields exam, evaluate for nuchal rigidity, scalp tenderness and Horner’s syndrome WORKUP: ESR if patient >60 (corrected ESR = (age + 10 if female)/2) PLAN: Dictated by patient’s history and physical Differential: 1) Meningitis: diffuse, photo/phono, meningismus, N/V, fever, malaise, irritability 2) Encephalitis: like meningitis, plus altered mental status, seizures, focal features 3) Subarachnoid hemorrhage: sudden-onset, WHOL, 10/10, meningismus 4) Pseudotumor cerebri (BIH): diffuse, photo, tinnitus, vision loss, VI palsy + wt gain/Vit A/meds 5) Carotid dissection, periorbital/auricular throbbing (carotidynia), Horner’s, TMB, focal features, double lumen on CTA; crescent sign on MRA with fat sats 6) Temporal arteritis: achy, jaw claudication, myalgias, ESR > age/2 + 10, wt loss 7) Hypertensive encephalopathy: frontal/occipital, throbbing, +/- confusion 8) Venous sinus thrombosis: diffuse or focal, seizure, meningismus 9) Optic neuritis/myositis: painful eye movements +/- vision loss, F>M 10) Migraine: moderate unilateral, F>M, pulsatile, photo/phono, n/v, transient visual obscurations, scintillating scotomas, fortification spectra, + family history, + menstrual, + triggers. Subtypes: common (no aura, 50%), classic (aura, 50%), complicated (focal features, <5%), retinal (monocular vision loss), vertiginous, acephalgic (aura without headache) 11) Cluster: severe unilateral, M>F, daily x wks-mths, photo/phono, autonomic features (Horner’s, tearing, rhinorrhea) 12) Tension-type: bilateral, pressure, “hatband”, shoulder/neck tightness 13) Occipital Neuralgia: unilateral over occipital region, to eye, lasts seconds 14) Paroxysmal Hemicrania: like clusters in women, indomethacin-reponsive 15) SUNCT syndrome: “short-lasting, unilateral, neuralgiform, conjunctival injection and tearing” Workup: R/O meningitis/encephalitis: HCT  LP (determine aseptic/viral/bacteria/fungal, etc.), broad tx R/O SAH: HCT  LP (HCT less likely to see blood after 48-72 hours) R/O Pseudotumor: HCT  LP (check OP > 20, decubitus position, legs extended after tap better) R/O dissection: MRI/A neck c/s gad with T1 fat sats, add MRI brain to r/o accomp infarction R/O temporal arteritis: TA bruit/tenderness/pulselessness, if ESR hi  steroids, bx next day R/O hypertensive encephalopathy: watch BP, if focal or AMS  MRI to see RPLS R/O VST: HCT with contrast (“empty delta sign”) vs MRV R/O optic neuritis/myopathy: HCT  MRI brain/orbits c/s gad, CK, admit for IV steroids for ON R/O acute migraine – tension-type continuum: consider HCT if new HA syndrome

title: Acute Manifestations of Parkinson Disease
text: MOVEMENT DISORDER EMERGENCIES ACUTE PSYCHOSIS IN PARKINSON’S DISEASE Diagnosis 1. Exclude secondary causes including infection (UTI, PNA,etc), metabolic derangements, medications. Treatment 1. Treat secondary cause if one exists. 2. Gradually reduce exacerbating PD medications (amantadine > dopamine agonists > COMT/MAOI > levodopa) 3. Cautious symptomatic treatment of psychosis with the least D2 blocking agents [quetiapine > clozapine]. PARKINSONISM-HYPERPYREXIA SYNDROME Signs and symptoms 1. Indistinguishable from Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome and often due to abrupt w/d of dopaminergic agents. Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: infection, metabolic derangements, medications (esp. dopamine blockers). Treatment 1. Restart dopaminergic agents. 2. If no PO access, establish PO access with temporary gastric tube (Nasogastric, Duotube). a. OK to crush Sinemet IR. May need to temporarily replace Sinemet CR with IR. (dose of IR is approx. ½ of CR dose) 3. If unable to use gut, may temporarily use apomorphine SC if available and only if necessary. a. Starting dose 2mg SC, max 5 doses per day. pretreatment with trimethobenzamide to avoid nausea. INPATIENT OR PERIOPERATIVE TREATMENT OF PARKINSONS DISEASE Treatment 1. Restart dopaminergic agents. 2. If no PO access, establish PO access with temporary gastric tube (Nasogastric, Duotube). a. OK to crush Sinemet IR. May need to temporarily replace Sinemet CR with IR. (dose of IR is approx. ½ of CR dose) 3. If unable to use gut, may temporarily use apomorphine SC if available and only if necessary. a. Starting dose 2mg SC, max 5 doses per day. pretreatment with trimethobenzamide to avoid nausea. GENERAL RECOMMENDATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF MOVEMENTS DISORDERS Robottom et al. Arch Neurol. June 2011.

title: Altered Mental Status Workup
text: ALTERED MENTAL STATUS History:  Onset  Precipitating factor  Baseline level of functioning (history of dementia?)  Recent infectious symptoms (dysuria, nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, cough)  Symptoms of CNS infection (neck stiffness, headache, nausea)  Access to toxic ingestion or illicit substances including alcohol  Associated focal deficits or abnormal movements Exam: full general exam with attention to signs of intoxication, dehydration and infection, neurologic exam including MMSE and with close attention to level of consciousness, attentiveness, and focal deficits. Check and document meningeal signs, asterixis and subtle signs of seizure. Differential: o Drugs (most common, illicit or prescribed) o Endocrine, Electrolytes, Ethanol, Emotional, Eyes/Ears o Low O2, Lack of drugs, Liver o Infection (second most common, esp in elderly) o Retention o Intracranial (stroke/hemorrhage/mass) o Uremia, Under-nutrition, Under-hydration o Metabolic Often two hits: 1) Baseline brain disease (stroke/PD/dementia), advanced age, sensory impairment (absence of hearing aid/glasses), psychiatric illness, pain, absence of orienting devices (eg, clock) PLUS 2) systemic/neurological issues noted above (toxin, infection, metabolic derangement, etc). Workup: determined by above but typically includes  Routine labs, consider TSH, vitamin B12, VDRL, ammonia, thiamine o Hyper/hypoglycemia, hyper/hypoNa+, hyperCa++, uremia, hepatic enceph, hypercarbia, hypothyroidism  Urine toxicology screen and alcohol level, review medication list for precipitants o Benzos, opiates, antihistamines, anticholinergics, neuroleptics, DA agonists, AEDs, lithium, TCAs, theophylline, digoxin, steroids, amantadine, H2 blockers, anti-emetics, INH, salicylates, FK506, quinilones, etc…  Urinalysis, culture, and chest x-ray  Non-contrast CT head +/- CTA, MRI for focal deficits o Consider diffuse cortical hypoperfusion or infarcts, R parietal infarcts, B thalamic infarcts (top- of-the basilar syndrome), brainstem infarct; edema/mass effect 2nd to stroke/tumor, carcinomatous meningitis, SDH  +/- LP if fever, leukocytosis, meningeal signs/symptoms, or any concern for HSV encephalitis  EEG to rule-out seizures or non-convulsive status and assess degree of encephalopathy Plan:  Determine and treat underlying cause, which is often multifactorial  Treat with IV thiamine empirically if malnourished or alcohol history (500 mg q8h x 3 days and then 100 mg daily)  Recommend supportive measures: reorientation, encourage sleep-wake cycles, use visual and hearing aids Rapidly Progressive Dementia Workup: *These can be some of the most complicated cases you will see in the hospital and you will often exhaust every test imaginable searching for an etiology, but here is a basic “starter kit” to work off of: 1. Serologic studies: CBC, Comprehensive metabolic panel (including Ca, Mg, and PO4), liver panel, ammonia, RPR, ESR, CRP, ANA, TSH, Free T4, vitamin B12, Lyme antibody, and HIV. 2. Urinalysis 3. CSF analysis: cell count and differential, protein, glucose, VDRL, immunoglobulin G (IgG) index, and oligoclonal bands 4. MRI brain w+w/o Gad 5. EEG

title: Brain Death
text: Determination of Brain Death Prerequisites (all must be checked)  Coma, irreversible and cause known  Neuroimaging explains coma  CNS depressant drug effect absent (if indicated toxicology screen; if barbiturates given, serum level < 10 ug/mL)  No evidence of residual paralytics (electrical stimulation if paralytics used)  Absence of severe acid-base, electrolyte, endocrine abnormality  Normothermia or mild hypothermia (core temp > 36C)  Systolic BP > 100 mm Hg  No spontaneous respirations Examination (all must be checked)  Pupils nonreactive to bright light  Corneal reflex absent  OCR absent (only test if C spine integrity ensured)  Oculovestibular reflex absent  No facial movement to noxious stimuli at supraorbital nerve, TMJ  Gag reflex absent  Cough reflex absent to tracheal suctioning  Absence of motor response to noxious stimuli in all four limbs (spinally mediated reflexes permissible) Apnea testing (all must be checked)  Pt is hemodynamically stable  Ventilator adjusted to provide normocarbia (PaCO2 35–45 mm Hg)  Patient preoxygenated with 100% FiO2 for >10 minutes to  PaO2 >200 mm Hg  Patient well-oxygenated with a positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) of 5 cm of water.  Provide oxygen via a suction catheter to the level of the carina at 6 L/min or attach T-piece with continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) at 10 cm H2O.  Disconnect ventilator  Spontaneous respirations absent  Arterial blood gas drawn at 8–10 minutes, patient reconnected to ventilator  PCO2 ≥60 mm Hg, or 20 mm Hg rise from normal baseline value. OR  Apnea test aborted Ancillary Testing To be performed only if clinical examination cannot be performed due to patient factors, or if apnea testing inconclusive or aborted. Only one needs to be performed.  Cerebral angiogram  HMPAO Spect  EEG  TCD  Document time of death (i.e. time of apnea confirmed by diagnostic ABG, or result of ancillary test if applicable)

title: Cardiac Arrest
text: CARDIAC ARREST TTM (Targeted Temperature Management) ALERT: Activated when a patient has suffered cardiac arrest and therapeutic hypothermia may be indicated to decrease the degree of neurologic injury. Neurology consult (staffed by Neuro-ICU Consult/Blue attending) should respond immediately. Protocol: o Determine if the patient is a candidate for therapeutic hypothermia (comatose, VT/VF/PEA/asystole). o A patient may not be a good candidate if o following commands or moving appropriately to noxious stimulation (if any uncertainty, would err on the side of cooling) o Refractory hemodynamic instability o Severe coagulopathy or uncontrolled bleeding (e.g. DIC, severe thrombocytopenia/liver failure, vaso-occlusive disease [e.g. Hgb-SS]); o Other, more likely, causes for coma o Significant pre-existing neurologic impairment (e.g. baseline MRS>= 4) o End-stage terminal illness (e.g. pre-arrest life expectancy < 6 months)  Obtain noncontrast CT head to rule out intracranial hemorrhage, which is a contraindication for hypothermia. N.B. in patients with a clear cardiac/pulmonary cause of arrest, the head CT should not delay the start of therapeutic hypothermia.  Arrange for stat long-term EEG as indicated. Document the following things in your note:  date/time of initial arrest (if known)  time passed until CPR was performed  date/time of restoration of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)  date/time of arrival in the hospital  date/time of your neurologic exam, if sedating or neuromuscular blocking agents were administered, and if the patient is intubated  coma exam  In your assessment and plan, please include the Arctic Sun EEG Protocol.  Document FOUR SCORE in all your notes (use the template). Also GCS (Glascow Coma Score) Neurology will arrange for critical care EEG for 3 days: initiation of cooling (day 1), rewarming (day 2) and rewarmed (day 3). If convulsive or non-convulsive status epilepticus is suspected based on physical exam, please call the neurology consult service. Algorithmic Approach to Post-cardiac Arrest Patients: 0-24 hours: Clinical examination (esp pupils/corneals); CT, MRI if CT uninformative 24-48 hours: Clinical examination, continuous EEG, consider neuron-specific enolase (NSE) 48-72 hours (post-arrest or post-rewarming): Clinical examination, continuous EEG, consider NSE 72 hours (post-arrest or post-rewarming): Clinical examination, SSEP, CT, consider NSE, consider MRI if CT unrevealing

title: Convulsive Status Epilepticus
text: UF CONVULSIVE STATUS EPILEPTICUS PROTOCOL (ADULT) Immediately • STAT 4mg IV Lorazepam IVP over 2 min., repeat dose if still seizing (total 0.1 mg/kg) • Monitor for hypotension and respiratory depression • Order STAT EEG • Call SWAT 5-30 min • FOS 20 mg PE/kg at rate of 150 mg PE/min (slow rate if hypotension occurs). Maximum dose = 2000 mg • Free PHT level 2 hours after load (goal free level of 2-3) • If at goal, start maintenance fosphenytoin 6 PE mg/kg in three divided doses • If not at goal, redose using calculation: 0.8 x (weight in kg) x (desired - actual level) x10 = mini-load in mg; can round to nearest 250 mg • Monitor for hypotension and cardiac arrythmias • Call NeuroCCM fellow phone (352 745 1912) for NICU bed • Convert STAT EEG to long term video EEG monitoring • Load with IV Keppra (60 mg/kg, max dose 4500 mg) • Call Epilepsy attending and Neurocritical Care attending for further management recommendations • If convulsive seizures continue, intubate and bolus with MDZ 0.2 mg/kg IVP • If still seizing, can repeat boluses of MDZ 0.2 mg/kg every 5 minutes (up to max dose of 2 mg/kg). • Start continuous MDZ infusion at 0.1 mg/kg/hr and increase rate by 5-10 mg total every 15 minutes as needed until convulsions stop (typical range 0.2-0.6 mg/kg/hr). Max dose is 2.9 mg/kg/hr. Titrate MDZ drip to electrographic seizure cessation on EEG. • Notify Epilepsy attending • After titrating MDZ drip, if still having convulsive seizures, load with depakote, phenobarbital (may also use other agents). Must be coordinated with Neurocritical Care, with further management to be determined on a case by case basis based on etiology, comorbidities and other potential risk of complications. *This protocol is ONLY for convulsive status epilepticus and does not include non-convulsive status or epilepsia partialis continua. Important practice points:  LZP = lorazepam  MDZ = midazolam  FOS = fosphenytoin  This protocol is contingent on weight-based dosing, make sure ED weights patient upon arrival.  Make sure that timing of doses, infusion rate, and actual doses are accurate prior to order.

title: Delirium Management
text: MANAGEMENT OF THE AGITATED PATIENT Agitation happens for a variety of reasons in the inpatient setting including psychosis, toxidromes, delirium, substance withdrawal, or rational people deciding they no longer want to conform to hospital restrictions. With agitation, it is always advisable to back off patients if you feel unsafe, involve other staff, and call security if the situation escalates. Restraints, both chemical and physical, are often necessary both for preventing patient and staff harm. General strategies: 1) Avoid/remove aggravating factors, 2) identify/treat cause, 3) supportive care, 4) control dangerous/disruptive behavior. Prevention: orientation protocol, structured activities, reassurance, sitter (ideally family). Visual/hearing aids. Nonpharmacological sleep aids, maintain day/night cycle (quiet time at night), pain management, early mobilization. Avoid: frequent change of environment (eg, room changes), restraints, constipation, anticholingerics, catheters Pharmacologic interventions have varying efficacy based on etiology, in general the following apply: 1) Delirious patients: (decreased sensorium, poor attention), benzodiazepines and anticholinergic medications should be avoided. In these individuals, antipsychotics should be first line. Often low dose haldol (0.5 to 2 mg) IM, PO, or IV can be helpful. A crude guideline is to monitor response to the first dose and treat accordingly. If improvement is seen you may think about halving the dose, if no change is seen you may repeat the dose after some interval (1 - 2 hrs), or if the situation worsens you may think about doubling the dose. The generally accepted max effective dose per 24 hr interval is around 15 mg. Watch for QT prolongation by monitoring with EKG once the patient is compliant. Common management strategies are to use standing haldol in delirium, with 0.5 - 1 mg Q12 being used frequently and evidence based, albeit weakly. 2) Withdrawal: For individuals thought to be in sedative withdrawal or with acute toxidromes, including cocaine intoxication, benzodiazepines are first line for agitation. Features suggestive of withdrawal include tremor, diaphoresis, self-reported craving, dysautonomia, history of daily RTC alcohol or benzodiazepine use, history of withdrawal. In these individuals, ativan is a good choice for agitation because it is available IM, IV and PO. Generally, treating in 1-2 mg doses with monitoring at fixed intervals is a safe strategy. 3) For individuals without delirium or withdrawal: an effective and safe strategy would be a combination of low dose haldol, ativan and bendaryl. Often a combination of haldol 2 mg, ativan 2mg, and benadryl 25 or 50 mg will sedate people quickly and safely. They can be draw together and given in one syringe if necessary. This combination can be given in stages if people are not responding after the first dose. Notably, the onset of action is similar for oral and IM preparations so patient cooperation is the deciding factor in choosing route of administration. 4) Extreme agitation: where the potential for mortal self harm is possible, benzodiazepines at high doses may be necessary, to the point of requiring intubation. 5) Drug induced extrapydramidal symptoms: Quetiapine (Seroquel) has a lower affinity for the D2 receptor and results in fewer parkinsonian symptoms than haloperidol. There is some evidence for its use in the treatment of delirium, but its use in acute agitation is limited by unavailability in parenteral forms.

title: DHE Protocol
text: Treatment of acute inpatient migraine 1. Migraine cocktail  IV fluids: 2-3 L NSS or 80-100 cc/hr while hospitalized  NSAIDS: 30-60 mg IV toradol (caution with GI sx)  DA antagonist: 10 mg metoclopramide or prochlorperazine ± 12.5 mg diphenhydramine to prevent akathisia 2 If pain persists (this is generally when ED calls), consider  Magnesium sulfate: 500 mg – 2g IV  Sodium valproate: 500 mg – 1g IV (caution in patients on topiramate, can cause hyperammonemia)  Methylprednisolone 200 mg – 1g IV (caution regarding avascular necrosis, diabetes, active infection)  DHE: See below. MUST discuss with attending beforehand. IV DHE Protocol Dihydroergotamine (DHE) is a serotonin agonist with neurologic effects. It is also a venoconstrictor; those with difficult IV access may require PICC line. DHE can be used to treat refractory migraine or cluster headache. - Counsel patient that when DHE protocol is complete that unfortunately there are no other inpatient therapies and discharge will occur even if headache hasn’t improved. Contraindications: peripheral vascular disease, CAD, stroke, severe or poorly controlled HTN, liver or renal impairment, pregnancy Orders: Admit to Neurology/telemetry floor bed, baseline EKG, weight, BMP, CBC, Mag, Phos, Coags, LFTs, UA, Urine tox, serum tox, serum alcohol panel, pregnancy test Protocol: Cumulative Dose = 3mg daily x 5 days Adult: age >16 and weight >50kg Pediatric: (adult dose) x (patient weight in kg) x (0.014) mg 8mg ondansetron, or 10mg metoclopramide, or 10mg prochlorperazine IV 30minutes prior to each DHE infusion Day 1: First Dose: 0.5mg in 1000mL normal saline over one hour Second Dose: - If well tolerated (including no nausea), 1 hour later give another 0.5mg in 250mL normal saline over one hour - If nauseated reduce dose to 0.25mg and give 8 hours later (with combination antiemetic therapy) - If headache completely resolved continue with 0.5mg 8 hours later - If severe nausea, chest pain, or hypotension occur discontinue DHE therapy Day 2-5: Third and subsequent doses if initial 2 doses were well tolerated: 1mg in 250mL normal saline over 1 hour every 8 hours for 10 doses - If headache has completely resolve treat for an additional 3 doses and then consider early discontinuation - If 0.25mg dose is required due to nausea, treat with combination antiemetic therapy 30 minutes prior to doses and test higher doses at 0.25mg increments Side effects: nausea, vomiting, leg cramps, limb pain/cramps, cool limbs, abdominal cramps, diarrhea, vasospasm, tachycardia or bradycardia, hypertension Side effect management:  slow rate of IV DHE infusion  reduce DHE dose to highest tolerable  increase dose of antiemetic, use combination antiemetic therapy  naproxen or benzo for muscle cramps Silberstein SD, Schulman EA, Hopkins MM. Repetitive intravenous DHE in the treatment of refractory headaches. Headache 1990; 6:334-9 Treatment of cluster headache: 100% O2 by face mask for 10 min, DHE protocol. Can also try lidocaine + steroid nerve block or Triptans. If prophylaxis is indicated, especially consider verapamil, lithium, and valproate.

title: Dizziness Evaluation
text: THE DIZZY PATIENT Patients use the term dizziness to describe a wide variety of symptoms: vertigo, presyncope, disequilibrium, nausea, diplopia, etc. Take the best history you can, but be at peace with the fact that most patients just can’t reliably tell you what they’re feeling (“I don’t know, doc, I just felt off”). Try to sort the rest out with a good neuro exam and the special maneuvers below. If all else fails, all dizzy people over the age of 60, especially with vascular risk factors, should be fully evaluated for an acute neurovascular event. Your task in the ED is to categorize the patient into one of three groups: 1) Benign Peripheral Positional Vertigo: History: lasts for less than 2 minutes, brought on by head movement Exam: Dix-Hallpike may produce classic mixed upbeating and torsional nystagmus towards the affected ear that starts after a delay (10-40 seconds - this takes patience during the exam!), then goes away after 30 seconds (“fatigues”), then the patient may feel spinning in the opposite direction upon sitting up; one side is usually much worse than the other. The response is less severe if you Dix-Hallpike them again (“fatigues”). Plan: Teach them the Epley, have them follow up with ENT if it doesn’t improve. If it really seems like BPPV without vertigo/nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike, teach them Epleys anyways; the otolith could also be in the horizontal canal, in which case the test and treatment are different 2) Vestibular system dysfunction (vestibular neuronitis, labyrinthitis): History: lasts for hours to days; labyrinthitis has hearing loss or tinnitus, vestibular neuronitis does not Exam: Nystagmus is mixed horizontal-torsional away from the affected side. Head thrust (see vestibulocular reflex below) – vestibular system dysfunction pt breaks fixation Plan: Fluids, meclizine, steroid taper. May need admission (to medicine preferably) because they will likely continue to vomit for several more days 3) Central vertigo: History: lasts hours to days, usually has associated symptoms (eg- dysarthria, diplopia, ataxia, weakness, etc) Exam: Central Nystagmus: Direction changing (eg. to right in right gaze, left in left-gaze), horizontal/rotatory, pendular, or vertical. There may be other brainstem or cerebellar findings on exam, but the absence of other symptoms cannot exclude a central cause. Use HINTS to INFARCT (sens 100%, spec 96%; Stroke 2009;40:3504) Tests (mnemonic: HINTS) (note, patient must be currently symptomatic for tests to be reliable) 1. Head Impulse – ask the patient to fixate on your nose and move his/her head rapidly 30o off midline and back to center. Look for corrective saccades. Test is normal in central vertigo, abnormal with peripheral vertigo (vestibular nerve dysfunction). 2. Nystagmus - evaluate in primary gaze and horizontal/vertical gaze in all directions. Benign etiologies have unidirectional nystagmus throughout, although the intensity may be different. Central lesions have horizontal direction change (eg left-beating in left gaze, right-beating in right gaze). Vertical nystagmus is always central. 3. Test of Skew – ask the patient to fixate on your nose and then cover/uncover each eye. If positive, you will see vertical corrective saccade/refixation when moving from one eye to the other (horizontal movement doesn’t count) Test interpretation (mnemonic: INFARCT). Concern for stroke if one or more of the following is true:  Impulse Normal  Fast-phase nystagmus Alternating  Refixation on Cover Test Plan: Admit as a presumed posterior circulation stroke. PICA infarctions can be subtle and can evolve over time. Be careful with use of MRI to identify these patients (especially in the ED) – hyperacute MRI may appear falsely negative in posterior fossa infarction

title: Epilepsy
text: SEIZURE CONSULT HISTORY: New onset seizure or known epilepsy? If known epilepsy: goal is to determine AED regimen and compliance, seizure type/frequency and last seizure, epilepsy physician, any recent triggers (see below). Check AED levels and if low or they are non-compliant, re- load; if not, consider increasing AED dose. Ask about prior AEDs tried. If new onset seizures, obtain comprehensive first seizure history:  RISK FACTORS: history of febrile seizures, family history of seizures, prenatal and perinatal complications, developmental delay, history of head trauma, history of CNS infection, history of CNS mass/malformation, metabolic disorder, stroke (hemorrhagic>ischemic), drug/alcohol use, consanguinity  TRIGGERS LOWERING SEIZURE THRESHOLD: Medications, AED noncompliance, infection, metabolic abnormalities, toxic ingestion, menses, stress, sleep deprivation, EtOH/benzo withdrawal, fever, use of stimulants, hypoglycemia, electrolyte disturbance, hypoxia, acute medical/neuro illness  CHARACTERISTICS: aura (visual, auditory, olfactory, epigastric rising sensation, psychic (déjà vu, derealization, depersonalization, fear, anxiety), type of seizure (especially how its starts), eyes open or closed, lateral eye or head deviation, length of seizure, associated tongue bite or other injury, incontinence, post-ictal confusion, post-ictal weakness.  SUBTLE SEIZURE SIGNS: history of staring, automatisms, waking up incontinent or injured (tongue bite), myoclonic jerks. EXAM: Focus on mental status, post-ictal paralysis or other focal deficits, subtle signs of ongoing seizures such as abnormal movements, head or eye deviation, eyelid twitching, pupillary abnormalities or hippus; tongue bite/other injury, and meningeal signs ED WORKUP: AED levels (only carbamazepine, phenytoin, valproate and phenobarb levels are available acutely, but others can be helpful to establish compliance), non-contrast head CT (new seizures only!), can consider MRI with/without contrast (seizure protocol). Basic labs including electrolytes and CBC, infectious workup (UA, CXR), toxicology screen, LP if concern for meningitis PLAN:  AED: load and then start maintenance dose (do not start AEDs for an uncomplicated first seizure)  EEG: highest yield within 24-48 h. If the patient is not “back to baseline,” consider stat EEG.  MRI with and without contrast, seizure protocol if warranted  Ativan IV PRN seizure activity  Evaluate medication list for medications that lower the seizure threshold  Can be discharged from the ED if they only had 1-2 seizure, have returned to baseline, and have nothing in the above workup requiring admission.  Information patient that he/she cannot drive for ≥ 6 months per Florida state law  Refer to the Urgent Clinic follow-up or your continuity clinic via Epic. Not Returning to Baseline after a Seizure The two most common reasons patients don’t wake up are prolonged post-ictal periods and overly eager benzo administration … But don’t miss anything important:  Make sure the patient isn’t still seizing. Look for subtle abnormal limb or eye movement, fluctuating arousal or complete lack of improvement. 40% of patients with status epilepticus continue having subclinical seizures after convulsive seizures are controlled. Consider a stat EEG.  Get a head CT if you don’t have one yet  Consider LP if you have any concern for meningitis/encephalitis or SAH not seen on HCT  You may need to admit a patient overnight for prolonged postical period or benzo intoxication. If there is no medical reason they had a seizure (e.g. EtOH w/d, infection) they will likely need to be admitted to neurology.

title: Guillain Barre Syndrome (AIDP)
text: GUILLAIN-BARRÉ SYNDROME (AIDP)  History and exam o Recent infections – Post-vaccination, C. jejuni, flu, CMV, EBV, mycoplasma o Symptoms develop over days, can be asymmetric o Look for weakness (most often without sensory loss) in distal>proximal, loss of reflexes (though can be normal early) o Back pain is common, and can be sign of demyelinating radiculitis o Incidence of GBS will increase with age, age>50 are at higher risk for GBS  Admission orders o Depending on respiratory status, consider IMC versus ICU admission. o Address code status in the ED as the patient may need intubation. o Check NIF and FVC q2h x 12 h then q4h. Consider intubation if NIF < 20 or FVC < 15cc/kg (or 1 liter) or a rapid downward trend is observed. o Consider intubation: -20/30/40 rule: VC <20ml.kg (or 1 liter); MIP<30, MEP <40. Reduction in VC, MIP, MEP >30%, PO2 <70 mmHg on room air, PCO2 >50 mmHg w.acidosis o Order telemetry – high risk of tachyarrhythmias from autonomic instability. o Aggressive bowel regimen, close monitoring for toxic megacolon or aspiration (autonomic involvement) o Avoid beta-blockers because there is a risk of complete heart block. o Perform a bedside swallow to evaluate for aspiration risk o Order DVT prophylaxis including SCDs  Work-up o Perform a lumbar puncture. There should be albumino-cytological dissociation (protein much higher than cells). Can be normal if caught early o Check HIV, CMV, hepatitis panel, and Campylobacter in stool o Order an EMG/NCS - look for absence or prolonged ‘F’ waves or H-reflexes, conduction block, and temporal dispersion (but not always early in the disease!)  Treatment o IVIg 2 grams/kg divided in 4-5 days. o Check IgA level prior to administration of IVIg as it may cause anaphylaxis in patients with IgA deficiency. Give Acetaminophen and Benadryl prior to each IVIg. Peripheral neuropathies: EMG/NCS may help guide you  Sensory-Motor polyneuropathy: HbA1c, TSH, BUN/Cr, RPR, B12, ANA, ANCAs, Lyme, LFTs, SPEP, UPEP, SSA/B, RF, ESR, HIV, consider heavy metal screen (needs special navy blue top tube)  Sensory neuropathy: SSA/B, anti-Hu  Mononeuritis multiplex: HbA1c, Lyme ab, SPEP/UPEP, ANA, ANCA, ESR, RPR, HIV, hepatitis serologies, consider cryoglobulins Polyneuropathy and mononeuritis multiplex: not sure how to start your differential? Acute AIDP (GBS), AMAN (very fast, axonal motor-only GBS), vasculitis, porphyria, toxins (organophosphates, arsenic, thalidomide), tick, drugs, acute thiamine deficiency, critical care neuropathy Stepwise Vasculitis: PAN, Sjogren, Churg-Strauss, Wegener’s, hypersensitivity cryoglobulinemia, (mononeuritis) SLE, RA, isolated PNS vasculitis. DM. CIDP. Infectious: Lyme, HIV, HepC/ cryos, leprosy. Infiltration: (sarcoid (esp. b/l facial nerve), granuloma, leukemia/lymphoma) Predom motor lead, porphyria, hereditary, AIDP/CIDP, multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN), dysproteinemia Pure sensory paraneoplastic (anti-Hu), Sjogren, toxic (cisplatin, B6), HIV, anti-sulfatide Ab, Friedreich’s, idiopathic, hereditary (HSN) Predom autonomic AIDP, DM, amyloid * toxins concentrated in urine can affect the bladder worst (e.g. cisplatin) Small-fiber DM, amyloid, toxic (EtOH), hereditary, Tangier, Fabry, AIDS, idiopathic Demyelinating Acute: AIDP; Sub-acute: CIDP, lead, POEMs; Chronic: CIDP, CMT1, HNPP

title: Headache
text: ACUTE HEADACHE HISTORY:  New headache or history of headaches  Location, quality, severity  Onset (sudden or gradual)  Aura  Transient visual obscurations  Associated features (nausea/vomiting/photophobia/phonophobia/ diplopia/tinnitus)  What medications and how much have they taken for the headache  Worsening factors (e.g. lying down, standing up, straining)  Diurnal pattern (e.g. worse in the morning)  OCP or Vitamin A/retinoid use Chronic headache syndrome: ask about family history of headaches and screen for lifestyle factors: caffeine use, sleep, hydration status, frequency of analgesic use, relation to menses, stress, relation to weather New headache syndrome: screen for red flags such as recent head or neck trauma, chiropractor visits, fevers or chills, recent travel or sick contacts, altered mental state, neck stiffness, focal neurologic or visual deficits New headache in the elderly: screen for GCA symptoms such as fever, weight loss, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, proximal limb myalgias, constitutional symptoms EXAM: Vital signs with particular attention to blood pressure and temperature, neurologic exam including a fundoscopic and visual fields exam, evaluate for nuchal rigidity, scalp tenderness and Horner’s syndrome WORKUP: ESR if patient >60 (corrected ESR = (age + 10 if female)/2) PLAN: Dictated by patient’s history and physical Differential: 1) Meningitis: diffuse, photo/phono, meningismus, N/V, fever, malaise, irritability 2) Encephalitis: like meningitis, plus altered mental status, seizures, focal features 3) Subarachnoid hemorrhage: sudden-onset, WHOL, 10/10, meningismus 4) Pseudotumor cerebri (BIH): diffuse, photo, tinnitus, vision loss, VI palsy + wt gain/Vit A/meds 5) Carotid dissection, periorbital/auricular throbbing (carotidynia), Horner’s, TMB, focal features, double lumen on CTA; crescent sign on MRA with fat sats 6) Temporal arteritis: achy, jaw claudication, myalgias, ESR > age/2 + 10, wt loss 7) Hypertensive encephalopathy: frontal/occipital, throbbing, +/- confusion 8) Venous sinus thrombosis: diffuse or focal, seizure, meningismus 9) Optic neuritis/myositis: painful eye movements +/- vision loss, F>M 10) Migraine: moderate unilateral, F>M, pulsatile, photo/phono, n/v, transient visual obscurations, scintillating scotomas, fortification spectra, + family history, + menstrual, + triggers. Subtypes: common (no aura, 50%), classic (aura, 50%), complicated (focal features, <5%), retinal (monocular vision loss), vertiginous, acephalgic (aura without headache) 11) Cluster: severe unilateral, M>F, daily x wks-mths, photo/phono, autonomic features (Horner’s, tearing, rhinorrhea) 12) Tension-type: bilateral, pressure, “hatband”, shoulder/neck tightness 13) Occipital Neuralgia: unilateral over occipital region, to eye, lasts seconds 14) Paroxysmal Hemicrania: like clusters in women, indomethacin-reponsive 15) SUNCT syndrome: “short-lasting, unilateral, neuralgiform, conjunctival injection and tearing” Workup: R/O meningitis/encephalitis: HCT  LP (determine aseptic/viral/bacteria/fungal, etc.), broad tx R/O SAH: HCT  LP (HCT less likely to see blood after 48-72 hours) R/O Pseudotumor: HCT  LP (check OP > 20, decubitus position, legs extended after tap better) R/O dissection: MRI/A neck c/s gad with T1 fat sats, add MRI brain to r/o accomp infarction R/O temporal arteritis: TA bruit/tenderness/pulselessness, if ESR hi  steroids, bx next day R/O hypertensive encephalopathy: watch BP, if focal or AMS  MRI to see RPLS R/O VST: HCT with contrast (“empty delta sign”) vs MRV R/O optic neuritis/myopathy: HCT  MRI brain/orbits c/s gad, CK, admit for IV steroids for ON R/O acute migraine – tension-type continuum: consider HCT if new HA syndrome

title: Headache in Pregnancy
text: HEADACHE IN PREGNANCY Primary Headaches 1) Migraine headache: in general, retrospective studies have suggested that migraine frequency and severity decrease during pregnancy, particularly during the 2nd and 3rd trimesters. A prospective study of 49 patients showed 79% had complete remission of headaches by the third trimester. By one month postpartum, 50% had recurrence of migraine. 2) Tension Headaches: no change in frequency during pregnancy, but few data exist Secondary Headaches  Pseudotumor cerebri: see other section  Pre-eclampsia: new hypertension >140/90, proteinuria>300mg/dL; headache may precede development of serious neurologic sequelae (i.e. eclampsia with seizures)  Subarachnoid hemorrhage: incidence may be higher in pregnancy (between 0.9-5/10,000) and during the six weeks postpartum, risk of SAH increases 28-fold  Cerebral Venous Thrombosis: occurs in 1/2500-10,000 deliveries; especially likely if associated hypercoagulable states (protein C/S or antithrombin III deficiency, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome). Can occur up to 2-8 weeks postpartum, too!  Postdural Puncture/Nonpostdural puncture: can occur with epidural & spinal anesthesia (CSF leak, pneumocephalus; other complications-subdural hematoma, aseptic/septic meningitis) Workup: 1) Worrisome features: new-onset headache, change in pattern, visual changes, speech abnormalities, focal neuro signs/sxs, meningeal signs, trauma, seizure, history of malignancy 2) Detailed headache history & neurologic ROS; medications, diet 3) Vitals, neuro exam (including funduscopic exam) 4) Labs (urinalysis, pre-eclampsia labs) 5) Imaging: if you suspect SAH  non-contrast head CT (sensitivity ~90% in first 24 h of onset); it takes 50-100 head CTs to reach a concerning level of radiation exposure (5 rads!) if you suspect venous sinus thrombosis  MRI/MRV (although, if necessary, a CT venogram can be done). The National Radiological Protection Board advises against MRI in the first trimester, but there is no evidence to support the advisory. 6) LP: if imaging reveals no hemorrhage and the suspicion remains high, lumbar puncture is the only way to exclude SAH. Also indicated if suspicious of meningitis. Treatment in Pregnant Patients: What’s Safe? Usually decided by our OB/GYN colleagues… Category B – no human Category C - no human/ Category D - evidence of Category X - evidence of studies, but animal studies animal studies or animal risk to humans based on risk to fetuses outweighs show no risk studies show adverse effect animal or human studies any benefit, contraindicated acetaminophen, caffeine, aspirin, codeine, lorazepam, diazepam, ergotamine, ibuprofen, naproxen, hydrocodone, amitriptyline, imipramine, dihydroergotamine indomethacin, butorphanol, propoxyphene, butalbital, nortriptyline meperidine, oxycodone, sumitriptan, hydromorphone, morphine, prochlorperazine dichloralphenazone, Reglan promethazine, clonazepam  avoided in third trimester (metoclopramide), fluoxetine

title: ICP and Herniation Management
text:
IV fluids: 0.9% normal saline to maintain euvolemia
NPO for angiogram OR bedside swallow study and diet, formal swallow study if needed
Continue necessary home medications; hold all antithrombotic medications
Avoid Valsalva maneuver: use antiemetics, antitussives and stool softeners
Pain management: scheduled Tylenol/acetaminophen 1g q6h if no liver disease. Codeine 30-60 mg q 6 hours PRN OR Ultram (tramadol) 25-50 mg q 6 hours PRN IF patient does not have seizures
In case of seizures: Keppra (levetiracetam). (try to avoid fosphenytoin - poorer outcomes)
DVT prophylaxis: SCDs, no heparin
GI prophylaxis: PPI (e.g., pantoprazole, omeprazole or lansoprazole [if gtube])
Consult PMR/Speech therapy as indicated
Code status Modified Fisher Scale for World Federation of Hunt and Hess vasospasm prediction Neurological Surgeons 0=no subarachnoid blood I=GCS 15, no motor deficit 0=unruptured aneurysm 1=thin focal or diffuse SAH; no II=GCS 13-14, no motor deficit 1=asymptomatic or mild HA and intraventricular blood nuchal rigidity 2=thin focal or diffuse SAH; III=GCS 13-14, motor deficit 1a=no meningeal signs but have intraventricular extension Present fixed neurologic deficit 3=thick focal or diffuse SAH; no IV=GCS 7-12, motor deficit 2= CN palsy, mod/severe HA, intraventricular blood present or absent nuchal rigidity 4=thick focal or diffuse SAH; V=GCS 3-6, motor deficit 3=mild focal deficit, confusion or intraventricular extension present or absent drowsiness 4=stupor, mod/severe hemiparesis 5=deep coma, decerebrate posturing, moribund appearance **For Hunt and Hess - add one grade for severe vasospasm on angiogram or presence of serious systemic disease (HTN, DM, COPD, severe atherosclerosis) Events/complications after aneurysmal SAH Early Late Chronic Rebleeding Cerebral vasospasm Chronic hydrocephalus that may require placement of a shunt Acute hydrocephalus Delayed cerebral ischemia Behavior, personality, memory changes Seizures Pituitary dysfunction Need for further retreatment for recurrent or residual anerusym Cardiopulmonary issues and stress Infection related to surgery or induced cardiomyopathy ventriculostomy Sodium and electrolyte abnormalities Acute elevations in ICP and management of elevated ICP: Sx of elevated ICP include: Non-focal: nausea, vomiting, hiccups, increased yawning, agitation, headache, diminished level of consciousness, Cushing’s response (elevated BP, bradycardia, respiratory depression), If any concern for worsening or impending herniation: Clinical suspicion or sustained (>5 min) elevation of ICP > 22 mmHg • Neurocritical care fellow: 352-745-1912 • Ensure appropriate vascular access, consider need for additional PIV and/or stat central line, transfer to ICU if on floor/IMC

Herniation syndromes Subfalcine • Infarct in distal ACA territory  ipsilateral cingulate gyrus migrates under anterior falx  ipsilateral leg weakness Central • Ascending: compression of PCA and SCA against tentorium • Descending: progression of abnormal flexor posturing to extensor posturing  impending death due to damaged respiratory and cardiac centers in medulla by herniated cerebellar tonsils through foramen magnum Cerebellar tonsillar • PICA, vertebral artery, anterior spinal artery infracts  infarct to brainstem, tonsils, and lower cerebellum, upper cervical cord  respiratory arrest, downbeat nystagmus Uncal • Compression of 3rd nerve against tentorial edge  constriction followed by ipsilateral pupillary dilation • Compression of calcarine branch of PCA  infarct in temporal or occipital lobe • Compression of sylvian aqueduct  obstructive hydrocephalus • Midbrain displacement can cause compression on contralateral cerebral peduncle / contralateral corticospinal tract  ipsilateral hemiparesis to compression (Kernohan’s notch phenomenon) Transalar (trans- • Descending / posterior variant: infarct in MCA territory from compression within sphenoidal) sphenoid ridge • Ascending / anterior variant: infarct in anterior and middle cerebral arteries from compression of supra-clinoid segment of ICA against anterior clinoid process Transcalvarial • Often after decompressive hemicraniectomy  compression of cortical vessels along bony margin predisposing to hemorrhagic infarct Management of herniation • Assess ABCs • Minimize noxious stimuli (treat subclinical seizures, glucose control, treat shivering) • Analgesia / sedation (control pain and agitation at the lowest effective doses (fentanyl, lorazepam, propofol) • Target normothermia Tier 0 • Head of bed ≥ 30º (unless unstable MAP or brain sag) (Standard) • Neck midline (optimize jugular venous drainage) • Iso or hyperosmotic fluids (NO D5W, ½ NS, etc) • Avoid and correct hyponatremia • Steroids in select conditions (brain tumor, abscess, non-infectious neuroinflammatory conditions) ⇩ • Hyperosmolar therapy • Temporary hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of 30-35 mmHg, consider 10 cc 1% lidocaine into Tier 1 ETT prior to suctioning • CSF drainage • Consider surgical decompression ⇩ • Hyperosmolar therapy for higher Na goals Tier 2 • Optimize sedation and analgesia • Rescue decompressive surgery should be considered as a life saving intervention ⇩ • Sedation titrated to ICP goal or burst suppression of cEEG (pentobarbital coma) • Hyperventilation to achieve mild to moderate hypocapnia (PaCO2 25-34 mmHg), short lived, <24 hours, works by ↑ vasoconstriction and ↓ cerebral blood volume. Effects at the Tier 3 expense of ↓ CBF therefore used only as bridge to urgent surgical therapy. Not recommended for routine management. Can be bridge to OR in actively herniating patients • Moderate hypothermia (target core temperature 32-34ºC)

patient should NOT be hyponatremic (<130)) • If persistent hypotension with bradycardia • Peripheral or central line after bolus  norepi gtt Target • Initiation: 0.5-1 ml/kg/hr • Have nurse repeat all Na samples with osmolality • Infusion rate not exceed 125 ml/hr dramatic changes as they frequently are (q4-12h): 3% gtt (513 mmol/L): monitor Na q2-4h inaccurate 300-320 • Severe edema or baseline sodium far from target (131-140) • If too quick drop during discontinuation, mOsmol/L • Central line restart previous solution at prior rate or higher as indicated • Initiation: 0.5 ml/kg/hr with/without a 25 – 50% decrease • Peripheral up to 48 hours at rate <50 cc/hr – discuss with NCC first • Rebound edema can occur on d/c, observe in ICU ~24 hours Discontinuation: Decrease rate by 50% every 4-6 hours, once rate is 20 ml/hr, it can be stopped

title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension
text: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension Headache disorder in which signs and symptoms suggest elevated intracranial pressure (headache, papilledema, vision loss). These patients will have normal CSF profile and no other cause of intracranial hypertension evident on neuroimaging. It typically affects women of childbearing age who are overweight (but this is not always the case!). This is a common consult in the ED and your task is to differentiate IIH from other headache disorders and determine if the patient needs admission. History • New headache or history of headaches • Location, quality, severity • Worse when lying down, sitting up , or straining? • Onset (sudden or gradual?) • Vision loss/blurriness/diplopia/transient visual obscurations—is this precipitated by position changes? • Aura • Associated with nausea/vomiting/pulsatile tinnitus/photophobia/phonophobia • What medications and how much have been taken for headaches • OCP or vitamin A/ retinoid use • Diurnal pattern (worse in morning or at night?) • Retrobulbar pain or back pain? • Questions targeted towards CNS infection: fevers, chills, altered mental status, seizures, sick contacts, recent travel, current systemic illness, concurrent use of immunosuppressing agents Differential -Migraine -Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis -Impaired CSF absorption via arachnoid granulations that can be due adhesions due to bacterial, viral, or fungal meningitis or SAH -Malignant hypertension -CNS mass lesion such as tumor or abscess -Obstructive hydrocephalus Exam Vitals signs with attention to blood pressure. Full medical and neurological exam with attention to meningeal signs, fundoscopy, visual field testing, cranial nerves (can see abducens nerve palsy in IIH). Imaging Findings: MRI/CT findings that suggest IIH but are not diagnostic for this condition : empty sella, flattening of the posterior aspect of the globe, distension of perioptic subarachnoid space (with or without tortuous optic nerves), transverse venous sinus stenosis Workup -MRI/ MRV w/wo contrast is best for evaluation, however oftentimes in ED CTA/CTV are obtained -Ophthalmology consult in ED for dilated fundoscopy exam to confirm papilledema -LP. Opening pressure >250mm H2O with patient lying on their side with legs extended confirms elevated intracranial pressure Treatment -Weight loss -First line: Acetazolamide (Diamox) start at 500mg BID. Discuss risks/benefits in patients with sulfa allergies -Topiramate (Topamax) start at 25mg and uptitrate in 25mg increments weekly until max dose 100mg daily -Furosemide 20-40mg QD for adults -Surgical procedures: optic nerve sheath fenestration, ventriculoperitoneal shunting, venous sinus stenting -Patient will need to follow up with neurology and ophthalmology following discharge from hospital or ED

title: Intracerebral Hemorrhage
text: INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE Intraparenchymal hemorrhage  Hypertension most common etiology – subcortical/brainstem  Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (cortical)  Arteriovenous malformation  Neoplasm (primary central nervous system, metastatic, leukemia)  Hematologic (sickle-cell disease, neoplasm, thrombocytopenia) Moyamoya disease  Drug use (warfarin, DOACs, amphetamines, cocaine, phenypropanolamine)  Iatrogenic (peri-procedural) Subarachnoid hemorrhage  Basilar cisterns: cerebral aneurysm until proven otherwise, differential includes trauma, other vascular malformations  Isolated high convexity: trauma, cerebral amyloid angiopathy, RCVS ICH Volume estimation = (A x B x C)/2 A = Diameter, B = Diameter perpendicular to A C = Number of slices on CT multiplied by slice thickness in cm ICH scores and 30-day mortality 0 points: 0% 1 point: 13% 2 points: 26% 3 points: 72% 4 points: 97% 5 points: 100% 6 points: 100% Control BP: Goal SBP 100-140  Use labetalol 10-20 mg IVP q1h PRN or nicardipine drip (start a 5 mg/h and titrate up as needed). If a nicardipine drip is started, ask ED resident/attending to place arterial line. A PIV can only be used for 24h.  The emergency room is busy. Do not rely on the nurse or ED resident to monitor the blood pressure. The neurology resident must alert the staff if SBP>140. Additional guidelines and workup:  ABCs, intubation if GCS<8  HOB > 30°  Reverse anticoagulation, if applicable (reversal protocol chart). Hold antithrombotics.  Discuss admission disposition with attending/fellow. o Neurology generally admits intraparenchymal hemorrhage unless neurosurgery is planned. o Subarachnoid hemorrhages go to neurosurgery unless clearly non-aneurysmal.  Admit to NICU using “STROKE PROGRAM: ICH ADMISSION ORDERS IP UF” order set  On admission, place SCD for DVT prophylaxis. Re-start heparin/LMWH after 24h or stable repeat imaging.  MRI brain (+/- contrast) unless obvious hypertensive etiology  CTA head vs. MRA head vs. conventional angiogram (unless hypertensive) – discuss with attending/fellow  Consider UDS, also blood cultures/TEE if concern for endocarditis

title: Lumbar Puncture
text:
LUMBAR PUNCTURE  Containdications: skin infection over puncture site, plt < 50K, INR>1.5, increased ICP (get prior head CT in age>60, immunocompromised, h/o CNS disease, abnormal neuro exam including poor mental status),  Equipment checklist (before you put on the sterile gloves): consent, kit, 22 gauge 5” needle, gauze, extra sample containers, gloves, mask, chlorhexidine swabs, IV meds if needed, assistance holding the patient if needed  Remember: signed consent in chart, bedside nurse at bedside, procedure time-out performed  Opening pressure: measured in the lateral decubitus position w/ legs straightened (bent legs can falsely elevate it)  Normal: 110 mm H2O in infants, 150 mm H2O in children, 180 mm H2O in adults and 250 mm H2O in obese adults  Low values usually due to prior LP and can be seen in spontaneous dural tears  High values due to hydrocephalus, idiopathic intracranial hypertension (i.e. pseudotumor), cryptococcal meningitis.  Protein: increased by 1 mg/dL for every 1000 RBCs  Isolated increase is a nonspecific abnormality seen in any process that disrupts the blood-brain barrier (e.g. diabetes complicated by cerebrovascular disease, and aging itself)  Increased without a corresponding elevation in cell count is Albumino-cytologic dissociation. This is the classic picture of Guillain-Barre.  Super high protein (in the 2 – 3 gram range) occurs in states of very low CSF flow with partial obstruction, often due to tumor  Glucose: should be about 2/3 of that in blood. Low values (Hypoglycorrhachia) are never good. Possibilities include tumor, bacterial meningitis, some fungal meningitis and sarcoidosis.  Cell count: Send the first and last tubes. RBCs will decrease between the two in traumatic taps, but remain high if there are really RBCs in the CSF. Determine the true number of CSF WBCs by subtracting 1 WBC for every 700 RBCs (assuming normal serum WBCs). Cells disintegrate in CSF that sits around for more than 4 hours before laboratory analysis, resulting in ‘pseudonormalization’ of CSF.  Micro: each test takes about 1cc of CSF. Many tests require approval. A good technique is to send a large sample to the lab and add on tests as necessary/approved.  Beware of possible false positive VDRL and EBV PCR and false negative HSV PCR if tap is traumatic  PCR testing should be done for suspected HSV1 and HSV2 DNA. (HSV2 can reactivate with fever and may be detected by PCR in the CSF of patients with other etiologies for encephalitis), also useful for VZV, enteroviruses, HIV-1 RNA, EBV DNA (seen in primary CNS lymphoma in HIV patients), West Nile virus DNA, CMV DNA, JC virus, and HHV-6 DNA. PCR is not useful for Lyme.  Intrathecal anti-borrelial antibody is currently the best test for CNS lyme testing.  Latex particle agglutination tests are available for many bacterial antigens: Neisseria, Streptococcus pneumoniae, H. influenzae type B, group B streptococci and E. coli – this is useful for rapid diagnosis in patients who have been pretreated and whose Gram stain and cultures are negative. These tests are highly specific and can be done within one hour, but they are not highly sensitive. A negative test does not exclude infection  No rapidly available PCR exists for bacterial DNA  Cryptococcal antigen testing is highly specific, sensitive and fast.  Other Studies  14-3-3 immunoassay for Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease can be falsely positive in HSV encephalitis, tuberculous meningitis and degenerative disorders. It should be interpreted in light of clinical symptoms and EEG abnormalities  CSF protein electropheresis (includes oligoclonal bands): done for suspected MS. Requires serum protein electropheresis for comparison (make sure blood sample is also sent). Bands are not specific for MS.

title: Movement Disorder Emergencies
text: MOVEMENT DISORDER EMERGENCIES ACUTE PSYCHOSIS IN PARKINSON’S DISEASE Diagnosis 1. Exclude secondary causes including infection (UTI, PNA,etc), metabolic derangements, medications. Treatment 1. Treat secondary cause if one exists. 2. Gradually reduce exacerbating PD medications (amantadine > dopamine agonists > COMT/MAOI > levodopa) 3. Cautious symptomatic treatment of psychosis with the least D2 blocking agents [quetiapine > clozapine]. PARKINSONISM-HYPERPYREXIA SYNDROME Signs and symptoms 1. Indistinguishable from Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome and often due to abrupt w/d of dopaminergic agents. Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: infection, metabolic derangements, medications (esp. dopamine blockers). Treatment 1. Restart dopaminergic agents. 2. If no PO access, establish PO access with temporary gastric tube (Nasogastric, Duotube). a. OK to crush Sinemet IR. May need to temporarily replace Sinemet CR with IR. (dose of IR is approx. ½ of CR dose) 3. If unable to use gut, may temporarily use apomorphine SC if available and only if necessary. a. Starting dose 2mg SC, max 5 doses per day. pretreatment with trimethobenzamide to avoid nausea. INPATIENT OR PERIOPERATIVE TREATMENT OF PARKINSONS DISEASE Treatment 1. Restart dopaminergic agents. 2. If no PO access, establish PO access with temporary gastric tube (Nasogastric, Duotube). a. OK to crush Sinemet IR. May need to temporarily replace Sinemet CR with IR. (dose of IR is approx. ½ of CR dose) 3. If unable to use gut, may temporarily use apomorphine SC if available and only if necessary. a. Starting dose 2mg SC, max 5 doses per day. pretreatment with trimethobenzamide to avoid nausea. GENERAL RECOMMENDATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF MOVEMENTS DISORDERS Robottom et al. Arch Neurol. June 2011.

title: Multiple Sclerosis
text: MS EXACERBATION (FLARE) Epi: expect 1-2 flares in untreated RRMS patient per year (75% will have at least 1 flare) Presentation: Relapse: patient-reported or objectively observed events typical of an acute inflammatory demyelinating event in the CNS current or historical, with duration of at least 24 hours, in the absence of fever or infection. Consider: vision loss with painful eye movements (optic neuritis), trigeminal neuralgia, focal weakness, focal numbness, ataxia, parasthesias, incontinence, falls, fatigue. KEY WORDS: NEW or WORSE; 24 hours or more; Symptom MUST be associated with FINDING on exam Pseudorelapse: acute worsening of symptoms that is typically associated with an increase in body temperature, which can be due to infection, exercise, or heat exposure. HISTORY:  Current symptoms (onset, duration, progression)  Past neurologic symptoms (if MS suspected)  Recent signs of infection (fever, chills, URI symptoms, dysuria)  If patient has known MS, have they ever had these exact symptoms before? This may suggest recrudescence due to systemic issue, such as an infection  Pregnancy If known MS, MS history:  When, where, and how they were diagnosed  Type of MS (RRMS, PPMS, SPMS, NMO)  Medication history and compliance, if on Tysabri, Gilenya or Tecfidera: LAST JCV TEST result  Last exacerbation, last round of steroids  If on Tysabri, date of last infusion. Watch out for Tysabri discontinuation rebound (usually around 3 months from last infusion)  Baseline deficits EXAM: Full physical and neurologic exam with specific attention to APD, optic nerve pallor, INO, Lhermitte’s sign, spasticity, and hyper-reflexia Workup +/- Admission + Treatment: 1) Rule out infection with UA/Ucx & CXR if indicated. If positive, may represent a “pseudoexacerbation” - treat the infection first and see if flare resolves. Patient may also warrant admission to medical service. 2) Rule out Uhtoff’s phenomenon (unmasking of old flare symptoms due to increased body temperature) - may not warrant acute steroid administration. 3) Consider admission based on following criteria – “can’t see” (optic neuritis), “can’t pee” (myelitis, neurogenic bladder), “can’t eat/swallow,” “can’t walk,” or “can’t take care of family.” If not admitted, can arrange outpatient infusion center IV steroids. Should also admit for first-time receiving IV steroids to monitor for paradoxical (psychiatric) reaction. 4) Start IV Solumedrol 1 g/d x 3-5 days. No need for oral taper if 5 days or less. 5) Prevent steroid side effects with GI prophylaxis (ranitidine 150 bid), finger stick glucose checks/sliding scale insulin, diabetic diet, and mental status checks. Consider a sleep aid. 6) Order PT/OT/Rehab service consults immediately if patient is unable to care for self at home on admission. 7) Continue outpatient symptomatic medications, such as muscle relaxants, SSRIs, stimulants, anticonvulsants, antipsychotics, amantadine, etc. Immunomodulating therapies (Interferons, Glatiramer acetate, Gilenya, Tecfidera, Aubagio) can be continued using home supply. Evidence for treatment in acute MS exacerbations  IV steroids may expedite the timing of recovery from a flare  IV steroids will not increase the degree of recovery or the course of disease  High-dose oral steroids may also expedite recovery, but IV treatment remains standard  Alternatives to failed IV steroids include IVIG and plasma exchange If suspected new diagnosis:  A full differential needs to be considered. Work-up: MRI brain and spine w/wo contrast, ACE (sarcoidosis), Lyme disease, anti-NMO Ab, RPR/VDRL, vasculitis and autoimmune panel, and lumbar puncture. CSF should be evaluated for CSF for basic studies and oligoclonal bands, myelin basic protein, IgG index, Lyme, ACE.

title: Myasthenia Gravis
text:
MYASTHENIA GRAVIS EXACERBATION Epi: 2:3 M:F, bimodal peaks – female 20s, males 50s, 20% bulbar only, 2/100,000 annual Presentation: facial diparesis, ptosis, neck extensor weak, nasal voice, hypotonic/weak * may be precipitated by med non-compliance, excessive activity, infection, or drugs1 * diff from “cholinergic crisis” – weakness, fasciculations, inc sweating/salivation, and miosis Workup + Admission (similar respiratory concerns as acute GBS) 1) Assess respiratory status with vital capacity (VC) and negative inspiratory force (NIF), follow q2 hours if worrisome, intubate if VC < 15cc/kg & trending downward. Pulse ox and ABG changes will lag hypoventilation! 2) Consider use of Bipap/non-invasive ventilation before intubation in myasthenia crisis, as it can prevent intubation unless patient is hypercarbic Consider intubation: -20/30/40 rule: VC <20ml.kg (or 1 liter); MIP<30, MEP <40. Reduction in VC, MIP, MEP >30%, PO2 <70 mmHg on room air, PCO2 >50 mmHg w.acidosis Estimated VC = counting aloud in one breath x 100 cc (ex. counts to 25 = 2.5 liters). If facial weakness, VC may be lower due to poor seal on apparatus. If patient vented for >7 days, consider trach and PEG  Intubation: Etomidate 0.2-0.3mg/kg, non-depolarizing paralytics if necessary (vecuronium)  Extubation: A Rapid Shallow Breathing Index (RSBI) of 100 predicts a 95% chance of failure of extubation. This is calculated by dividing tidal volume by respiratory rate. Also FVC >15 mL/kg, NIF >30 cmH2O, and PEF >40 cm H2O 3) Check CXR and cultures 4) Admit to NICU 5) Eliminate any medications that can exacerbate myasthenic symptoms (see list below) 6) If new diagnosis: - check acetylcholine receptor antibodies - order EMG/NCV with repetitive stim, consider single fiber - consider tensilon test - check thyroid function tests - CXR +/- chest CT to rule out thymoma 7) Acute treatment with IVIg and/or plasma exchange, plus initiation of acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (below). Start steroids, however steroids may acutely worsen symptoms. 8) GI and DVT prophylaxis 9) Order PT/OT/Rehab consults – may need Rehab or SNF Acute and Chronic Treatment: 1) IVIg or plasma exchange – see GBS section for orders. 2) Start Mestinon 30 PO TID, increase to 60-120 mg q4-6 hrs (maintenance), can increase to 120 mg q3 hrs (maximum). Long acting Mestinon (timespan) can be given at night for pts with nocturnal or early morning weakness. IV Mestinon dose is 1/30th of oral Mestinon. Use Robinul for excessive GI cholinergic effects, but watch for ileus/megacolon. Hold if patient is ventilated (secretions) 3) Long-term Rx: Prednisone, to consider Imuran, other immune suppressants, thymectomy (RCT proven benefit in all patients with generalized myasthenia, AchR binding Ab positive).  existing anticholinesterase medications should be stopped while patient’s on vent, due to increased secretions affecting weaning. Restart at ½ dose 1 day prior to extubation. Acute Tx Treatment Dosing Time to Onset Adverse Effects Plasma Exchange 1-1.5x plasma volume 2-3 treatments Coagulopathy, line infection, for 5 treatments qd or arrhythmia, shock qod IVIg 2grams/kg divided in about 5 days Aseptic meningitis, fluid 4-5 days overload
Chronic Tx Treatment Dosing Time to Adverse Effects Onset Mestinon 30-120mg q3-6 hrs Immediate GI disturbance, cramps, pulm edema (pyridostigmine) Prednisone 60-80mg/day with taper 2-4 weeks Osteroporosis, weight gain, 20 mg/day with titration hyperglycemia 5 mgQ3days Imuran (azathioprine) 1-3 mg/kg/day 3-9 months Leukopenia, hepatotoxicity Cellcept 1000mg BID Few months Anemia, leukopenia, diarrhea (mycophenolate mofetil) Drugs to avoid in myasthenia gravis: Bold indicates more significant effect Drug Class Specific Drugs Neuromuscular depolarizing agents Succinylcholine Calcium channel blockers Amlodipine, felodipine, nifedipine, verapamil Beta blockers Atenolol, nadolol, propranolol, sotalol, timolol Anti-arrhythmic agents Etafenone, peruvoside, procainamide, propafenone Quinolone Derivatives Chloroquine, quinidine, quinine Penicillamine Corticosteroids Hydrocortisone, methylprednisone, prednisolone, prednisone H2 receptor antagonists Cimetidine, ranitidine, roxatidine Antibiotics Aminoglycosides (amikacin, clindamycin, gentamycin, neomycin, streptomycin, tobramycin) Macrolides (azithromycin, erythromycin) Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, norfloxacin, oxfloxacin) Penicillins/ampicillin Polymixins Tetracyclines Imipenem Vancomycin Botulinum Toxin Magnesium sulfate Antiepileptics Carbamazepine Gabapentin Phenytoin Trimethadione Chemotherapeutic agents Cisplatin Doxorubicin Etoposide Interferons (alpha and beta) Psychotrophic medications Chlorpromazine Haloperidol Lithium Tricyclic antidepressants Antihelminthics Pyrantel pamoate

title: Neurocritical Care
text: NEUROCRITICAL CARE Intracranial Vascular Malformations Vascular Malformation Rupture Risk and Suggested Treatment Type Yearly Risk Factors Treatment rupture risk AVM 5% Size (>3 cm), deep venous Surgical, gamma knife embolization, drainage, nidal aneurysms, observation depending on location, size, prior hemorrhage age Cavernous 2-3% Prior hemorrhage Surgical vs observation malformation Venous angioma 0.1-0.5% Large size Not warranted Capillary Unknown – Not warranted telangiectasia (rare) Subarachnoid hemorrhage Thunderclap SAH, pituitary apoplexy, arterial dissection, venous sinus thrombosis, hypertensive headache ddx urgency, RCVS, intracranial hypotension, primary thunderclap headache Sulcal subarachnoid RCVS, venous sinus thrombosis, vasculitis, mycotic aneurysm, trauma, amyloid hemorrhage ddx angiopathy, meningeal neoplasm, dural AV fistula, meningitis Immediate Management in the ED To conform the diagnosis:  Non-contrast CT  LP if CT findings are normal (xanthochromia might be falsely negative if <6h from symptom onset) Follow up Management after confirmed SAH  Obtain CBC, electrolytes, Ca, Mg, PT/PTT, glucose  ECG and CXR to rule out neurogenic pulmonary edema or cardiac stunning  Keep SBP <160 mm Hg until aneurysm secured. Use labetalol 10 mg IV if HR >60, hydralazine 10 mg IV for HR <60  Neurosurgery consultation for angiography and/or EVD  EVD if patient has depressed LOC and hydrocephalus  Tranexamic acid 1 g IV if onset within past 72 hours; caution in patients with severe coronary artery  disease or who have a delayed presentation and may already be in vasospasm  Monitor for complications: rebleeding, hydrocephalus, vasospasm and hyponatremia due to cerebral salt wasting or SIADH  Call NICU consultant, admit to ICU  Bedside dysphagia evaluation to be performed by admitting resident. NPO if fails o Consider formal SLP assessment if facial weakness/significant dysarthria o No oral meds prior to bedside dysphagia screen Admission Orders 1. Include WFNS, Modified Fisher, Hunt and Hess scores in initial note (See tables below) 2. Admit to NICU unit 47 under neurosurgery service 3. Neuro checks per unit routine 4. Order STAT head CT if neurologic deterioration. 5. Blood pressure management: Labetalol 10 mg, every 15 minutes if HR >60, alternatively use hydralazine 10 mg for HR <60. For refractory HTN, initiate nicardipine infusion and titrate to effect. a. Unsecured aneurysm, keep SBP <160 mm Hg b. Consider inserting arterial line for dynamic monitoring 6. Continue TXA 1g q6h until aneurysm is secured (no more than 72h); caution in pts with severe cardiac disease 7. Nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours by mouth or NG tube for 21 days for delayed cerebral ischemia prophylaxis. Transition to 30 mg q2h if patient develops hypotension with typical dosing. 8. Monitor for elevated intracranial pressure 9. Discuss management with NCCM fellow and neurosurgery 10. Strict bedrest and strict I/Os, insert urinary catheter 11. Daily electrolytes to monitor for hyponatremia

title: Neuroimmunology
text: MS EXACERBATION (FLARE) Epi: expect 1-2 flares in untreated RRMS patient per year (75% will have at least 1 flare) Presentation: Relapse: patient-reported or objectively observed events typical of an acute inflammatory demyelinating event in the CNS current or historical, with duration of at least 24 hours, in the absence of fever or infection. Consider: vision loss with painful eye movements (optic neuritis), trigeminal neuralgia, focal weakness, focal numbness, ataxia, parasthesias, incontinence, falls, fatigue. KEY WORDS: NEW or WORSE; 24 hours or more; Symptom MUST be associated with FINDING on exam Pseudorelapse: acute worsening of symptoms that is typically associated with an increase in body temperature, which can be due to infection, exercise, or heat exposure. Work-up: MRI brain and spine w/wo contrast, ACE (sarcoidosis), Lyme disease, anti-NMO Ab, RPR/VDRL, vasculitis and autoimmune panel, and lumbar puncture. title: Neuroinfectious Disease
text: NEUROINFECTIOUS DISEASES MENINGITIS vs. ENCEPHALITIS (start treatment empirically, don’t wait for LP…): Meningitis Encephalitis S/S: HA, photo/phono/meningismus, N/V, fever, S/S: similar to meningitis, plus acute confusion, malaise, irritability personality changes, seizures Bugs: Bugs: HSV-1 (#1), arbo (SLE, LAC, EEE, WEE), Viral: echo, coxsackie, mumps, HSV-2, adeno WNV, VZV, legionella Bacterial: by age… <1m: GBS, E.coli, Listeria (“hear bells”) 1-23m: S.pneumo, N.mening, H.flu, E.coli 2-50y: N.mening, S.pneumo 50+y: S.pneumo, N.mening, Listeria, GNR W/U: HCT (cid:31) LP (routine, HSV PCR, t/c W/U: HCT (cid:31) if no abscess (cid:31) LP (routine, HSV PCR, crypto/TB/AFB/viral studies) t/c viral/toxo) (cid:31) t/c MRI and EEG Treatment: empirically… Treatment: by bug… Viral: supportive (if high suspicion for HSV, start HSV/VZV: acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8 x 14+d acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8) CMV: ganciclovir or foscarnet Bacterial: <1m cefotax/ceftriax + ampicillin Legionella: azithro, quinolone, tatracyclines 1-23m cefotax or ceftriax + vanco 2-50y ceftriax + vanco 50+y ceftriax + vanco+ ampicillin Fungal: amphoB + flucytosine + fluconazole TB: rifampin+INH+pyrazinamide+ethambutol, +/- streptomycin (“RIPES”) * if trauma: use cefepime or meropenem * Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days was shown to be beneficial in subgroups with pneumococcal meningitis, 1st dose 20 min before abx. Prognosis: Prognosis: - aseptic/viral: most improve over 2 weeks HSV& EEE = 70% die untreated, 25% treated - bacterial: 20% mortality (S. pneumo worst) Others = 5-20% mortality rate Tunkel AR et. al. Practice guidelines for the management of bacterial meningitis. Clin Infect Dis 2004 Nov 1;39(9):1267-84. de Gans et. Al. Dexamethasone in adults with bacterial meningitis. NEJM, 2002; 347(20): 1549-56. Serum Studies 1. Viruses: Measure IgM and IgG antibodies acute and convalescent (4 fold rise in IgG): St. Louis, West Nile, Eastern and Western Equine, Japanese encephalitis, Dengue fever EBV (VCA IgM and IgG and EBNA), VZV, HSV-1 (IgM), Rabies, HIV (RNA can be checked early when viral load high but ELISA weakly positive or negative) 2. Tick-borne bacteria: IgG & IgM by indirect immunofluorescence for RMSF, Lyme ELISA- confirmatory Western blot, Ehrlichia antibodies by indirect fluorescent antibody 3. Parasites: Toxoplasma gondii - No IgG or IgM Abs does not exclude toxoplasma encephalitis in AIDS patients - If +ve in HIV patient, tx with pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine and folinic acid. (Incidence reduced among people receiving prophylaxis with sulfadiazine or dapsone and pyrimethamine against Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia) b) If toxoplasma serology negative, check CSF EBV pcr and consider brain biopsy of any accessible mass lesion for lymphoma Taenia solium (cysticercosis): ELISA, but 50% with solitary lesions will be seronegative

title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome, Serotonin Syndrome, and Acute Dystonia
text: NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME (NMS) Signs and symptoms 1. Mental status change: agitated delirium, confusion, encephalopathy (82%) 2. Muscular rigidity: “lead pipe” tremor (up to 92%)  elevated CPK and myoglobinuric acute renal failure 3. Hyperthermia: >38°C (87%), >40°C (40%) 4. Autonomic instability: tachycardia (88%), labile BP (77%), tachypnea (73%), diaphoresis Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: acute drug intoxication, metabolic derangements, meningitis, etc. Treatment 1. Admit to ICU 2. Discontinue offending agent (e.g. neuroleptics, D2 blocking antiemetics or abrupt w/d levodopa) 3. Supportive care for Autonomic instability, DVT/PE prophylaxis, IVFs to prevent rhabdo 4. Symptomatic treatment: a. Agitation - Lorazepam 0.5-1mg prn (may also help with rigidity) b. Rigidity – Dantrolene 0.25-2mg/kg IV q6-12 hours (max dose 10mg/kg/day; be aware of hepatotoxicity) c. Fever – cooling blankets, ice lavage, acetaminophen SEROTONIN SYNDROME (SS) Signs and symptoms 1. Mental status change: agitated delirium, restlessness, disorientation 2. Neuromuscular hyperreactivity: tremor, rigidity, myoclonus, rigidity, hyperreflexia (+Babinski and clonus) 3. Autonomic instability: dilated pupils, diaphoresis, hypertension, diarrhea, vomiting Diagnosis 1. Exclude other etiologies: acute drug intoxication, metabolic derangements, meningitis, etc. Treatment 1. Admit to ICU 2. Discontinue offending agent (e.g. SSRIs, MAOIs, buspirone, trazodone, lithium, opiates, etc.) 3. Supportive care for Autonomic instability, DVT/PE prophylaxis 4. Symptomatic treatment: a. Agitation, Neuromuscular hyperreactivity - Lorazepam 0.5-1mg prn b. Cyptoheptadine (only for severe cases) 12 mg PO, then 2mg q2 hours until clinical response ACUTE DYSTONIC REACTION Signs and symptoms 1. Intermittent or sustained dystonic posturing due to blockade of D2 receptor (neuroleptics, certain antiemetics) Diagnosis 1. Review medication list/MAR for offending agent. 2. Exclude metabolic derangements (hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia) or other drug toxicity. Treatment 1. Discontinue offending agent 2. Diphenhydramine 25-50mg IV/IM vs Lorazepam 0.5-1mg IV (acute); Diphenhydramine 25mg PO qid (x48hr) 3. Alternative therapy: Benztropine 2mg IV/IM (acute); Benztropine 1-2mg PO bid (x48hr)

title: Neurologic Examination
text: THE NEUROLOGICAL EXAMINATION Mental Status Arousal state: Alert, Sleepy, Lethargic, Stuporous, Coma. Best to describe (e.g. opens eyes spontaneously vs tovoice vs sternal rub vs deep pain) Attention: spell WORLD backwards, months backwards, serial 7’s, count from 20 to 1 Language: Fluency, repetition, naming, reading, writing, comprehension (1-step midline vs appendicular commands vs 2-step vs 2-step reversed grammar (touch your nose after pointing to the ceiling) Fund of Knowledge- current events, quality of medical Hx Praxis: Show me how you use a hammer Memory: 3-5 objects at 1 minute, digit span (avg 7), story retelling. Distant memory. Visuospatial: Organization of space (clock drawing, complex figure copy) Neglect- extinction, line bisection, target elimination Executive: Organization of knowledge, inhibition, sequencing, perseveration Verbal fluency: name animals in 1 minute (>20 is nml) Oral trails (A1, B2, 3C….), Luria 3-step (fist, edge, palm) registration and repeat 3 times. Go/no go (e.g. raise your hand every time I say a letter, except when I say ‘D’) Cranial Nerves Fundus—disc/vasculature/venous pulsations/retina Visual acuity (pinhole to correct refraction), color vision (especially red desaturation) Visual fields (cut/neglect) Pupils- size, reactivity, ptosis Extraocular mm- saccades and smooth pursuit (ductions-both eyes, versions-one eye, alignment) Face sensation—LT/PP/temp, v1-v3 dist, corneal reflex Face strength— central vs peripheral, hyperacusis, dysgusia, corneal dehydration Hearing— hi/lo pitch, Weber, Rinne. Finger rub is usually enough. Vestibular- VOR, past pointing, Fukuda march test, Dix-Hallpike, nystagmus Palate elevation—Aaaah, gag, uvula position, myoclonus (CTT lesion) SCM strength/bulk—innervates ipsi SCM, contra trap Tongue—position, bulk, fasciculations, strength (tongue against cheek) Motor Bulk (atrophy, fasciculations) Tone (flaccid, spastic/clasp knife, rigid/lead pipe), abnl mvmt (freq, amp, rest, action, tremor) Strength: Drift, satelliting, isolated muscles (3=antigravity), functional tests- heel/toe walking, rise from chair with arms crossed, squat, deep knee bend Sensory Extremities/trunk/back/perineum. Look for a sensory level; Romberg Anterolateral system (temp/pp/some ft) Dorsal Columns (lt/vib/discrimination/proprioception/some ft) Cortical sensory signs- graphasthesia, extinction, stereoagnosia Coordination Midline/vermis: trunk/hemispheres: appendicular coordination FTN, HTS, titubation, optic ataxia, nystagmus, rhythm testing (RAM) Reflexes DTRs: 0 to 4+: 3= spread of reflex, not amplitude of response, Jaw jerk (to distinguish cord/brain) Frontal release (glabellar, rooting, snouting, palmomental, grasp) Hoffman/Babinski, clonus Gait Base of stance, Stability of stance (provocation), posture, Initiation of gait, Stepping, Base of gait, Arm swing, Turning strength and balance–tandem walk
