title: Neurotology
text: THE DIZZY PATIENT Patients use the term dizziness to describe a wide variety of symptoms: vertigo, presyncope, disequilibrium, nausea, diplopia, etc. Take the best history you can, but be at peace with the fact that most patients just can’t reliably tell you what they’re feeling (“I don’t know, doc, I just felt off”). Try to sort the rest out with a good neuro exam and the special maneuvers below. If all else fails, all dizzy people over the age of 60, especially with vascular risk factors, should be fully evaluated for an acute neurovascular event. Your task in the ED is to categorize the patient into one of three groups: 1) Benign Peripheral Positional Vertigo: History: lasts for less than 2 minutes, brought on by head movement Exam: Dix-Hallpike may produce classic mixed upbeating and torsional nystagmus towards the affected ear that starts after a delay (10-40 seconds - this takes patience during the exam!), then goes away after 30 seconds (“fatigues”), then the patient may feel spinning in the opposite direction upon sitting up; one side is usually much worse than the other. The response is less severe if you Dix-Hallpike them again (“fatigues”). Plan: Teach them the Epley, have them follow up with ENT if it doesn’t improve. If it really seems like BPPV without vertigo/nystagmus on Dix-Hallpike, teach them Epleys anyways; the otolith could also be in the horizontal canal, in which case the test and treatment are different 2) Vestibular system dysfunction (vestibular neuronitis, labyrinthitis): History: lasts for hours to days; labyrinthitis has hearing loss or tinnitus, vestibular neuronitis does not Exam: Nystagmus is mixed horizontal-torsional away from the affected side. Head thrust (see vestibulocular reflex below) – vestibular system dysfunction pt breaks fixation Plan: Fluids, meclizine, steroid taper. May need admission (to medicine preferably) because they will likely continue to vomit for several more days 3) Central vertigo: History: lasts hours to days, usually has associated symptoms (eg- dysarthria, diplopia, ataxia, weakness, etc) Exam: Central Nystagmus: Direction changing (eg. to right in right gaze, left in left-gaze), horizontal/rotatory, pendular, or vertical. There may be other brainstem or cerebellar findings on exam, but the absence of other symptoms cannot exclude a central cause. Use HINTS to INFARCT (sens 100%, spec 96%; Stroke 2009;40:3504) Tests (mnemonic: HINTS) (note, patient must be currently symptomatic for tests to be reliable) 1. Head Impulse – ask the patient to fixate on your nose and move his/her head rapidly 30o off midline and back to center. Look for corrective saccades. Test is normal in central vertigo, abnormal with peripheral vertigo (vestibular nerve dysfunction). 2. Nystagmus - evaluate in primary gaze and horizontal/vertical gaze in all directions. Benign etiologies have unidirectional nystagmus throughout, although the intensity may be different. Central lesions have horizontal direction change (eg left-beating in left gaze, right-beating in right gaze). Vertical nystagmus is always central. 3. Test of Skew – ask the patient to fixate on your nose and then cover/uncover each eye. If positive, you will see vertical corrective saccade/refixation when moving from one eye to the other (horizontal movement doesn’t count) Test interpretation (mnemonic: INFARCT). Concern for stroke if one or more of the following is true:  Impulse Normal  Fast-phase nystagmus Alternating  Refixation on Cover Test Plan: Admit as a presumed posterior circulation stroke. PICA infarctions can be subtle and can evolve over time. Be careful with use of MRI to identify these patients (especially in the ED) – hyperacute MRI may appear falsely negative in posterior fossa infarction

title: Neuro-ophthalmologic Emergencies
text: NEURO-OPHTHALMOLOGIC EMERGENCIES Acute Third Nerve Palsy  Start with a HCT to rule out subarachnoid blood or herniation.  Complete or partial ophthalmoparesis with pupil involvement requires some type of angiography to evaluate for a compressive aneurysm – start with CTA or MRA but then may still need conventional angiogram. You may also want to get MRI instead of/ in addition to CT scan depending on luxuries of time and resource.  Complete opthalmoparesis with spared pupil in patients over 50 with vascular risk factors is likely ischemic palsy, consider getting an MRI scan and then observing. Usually improve within 2-3 months.  Partial opthalmoparesis with spared pupil could be caused by an aneurysm, angiography is needed.  If in doubt with an acute 3rd nerve palsy, image and obtain angiography. Pituitary Apoplexy Symptoms: Classic syndrome: HA, sudden visual loss (both visual field and acuity), and ophthalmoplegia). May be accompanied by meningeal signs, hypopituitarism, unstable BP, encephalopathy, facial pain/numbness. Differential: Pituitary tumor infarction or hemorrhage and post-partum hemorrhage/Sheehan syndome. Management: CT scan may miss so MRI (pituitary-protocol) is needed. LP may demonstrate subarachnoid blood and aseptic meningitis. If MRI isn’t conclusive and there is SAH, you may have to get angiographic study to rule out aneurysm. Immediate therapy includes. steroids/hydrocortisone to prevent shock from acute hypopituitarism. Electrolyte abnormalities may be prominent and need to be addressed acutely. Giant Cell Arteritis Symptoms : HA, tenderness of scalp, temple or face of affected side(s), jaw claudication and current or past history of polymyalgia rheumatic, acute vision loss - anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION), can also have central retinal artery occlusion, posterior circulation infarction. Usually female (ratio 3:1) and over 60-70 (almost unheard of <50 yrs), may have premonitory symptoms of transient monocular blindness, bright light amaurosis, transient binocular diplopia and formed visual hallucinations. Exam: Look for scalp tenderness, visual acuity loss, chalky white disc edema, and cotton wool spots. If evidence of both central retinal artery occlusion and AION, you have likely sealed the diagnosis. Can have motility defects from ischemia of the extra-ocular muscles, nerves or a brainstem infarct Management – Check ESR - most will be above 40 mm/hr in giant cell arteritis. Consult vascular/ophtho for biopsy. Immediately treat with steroids - IV Solumedrol 1 g daily x 3-5 days then 1mg/kg per day PO prednisone. ***Do not wait for biopsy to start treatment! Ocular Cranial Nerve Palsies: General rules for localization:  Diplopia resolves upon covering one eye: if it doesn’t the problem is likely ocular or refractive.  On cover- uncover testing, an eye that is deviated out (exotropic) will move in when uncovered to pick up the fixation target (usually a finger or your nose)  Think INO, IIIrd n. palsy or exotropia related to medication effect. If the eyes move out with uncover (esotropia)  think of a sixth nerve palsy or abduction deficit.  Mimics of extraocular nerve palsies: thyroid eye disease, myasthenia gravis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia, decompensated phoria, orbital wall fractures, Wernicke’s syndrome, skew deviation, botulism, orbital tumors, Miller Fisher variant of Guillian-Barre Syndrome, mitochondrial d/o (CPEO: chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia, Kearns-Sayre Syndrome), vitamin E deficiency Afferent visual complaints: 1. History: monocular vs. binocular, pain, timing 2. Exam: acuity (best corrected with glasses and pinhole), color (especially red), visual fields (including blind spot), fundus, pupils (is there an APD) 3. Special tests: visual fields (Humphrey, Goldmann), OCT (quantifies retinal nerve fiber layer thickness), orbital ultrasound and the rest of the neurology favorites (MRI, LP etc.)

title: Nonconvulsive Status Epilepticus
text: UF NON-CONVULSIVE STATUS EPILEPTICUS PROTOCOL (ADULT) Immediately: • Order lorazepam STAT • First 5 min give 2mg lorazepam IVP over 2 min • If still seizing, then repeat dose of 1-2 mg to total of 0.1mg/kg • Watch for hypotension and respiratory depression • Order STAT EEG 5-30 min • Order STAT fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg at rate of 150 mg PE/min (slow rate if hypotension occurs) Maximum dose = 2000 mg • Order Free phenytoin level 2 hours after load. Goal free PHT level of 2-3 • If level at goal , start maintenance fosphenytoin 5 mg PE/kg in 3 divided doses per day • If level not at goal, redose using calculation: 0.8 x (weight in kg) x (desired - actual level) x10 = mini-load in mg • can round to nearest 250 mg • Monitor for hypotension and cardiac arrhythmias By 30 min • Call NeuroCCM fellow phone (352-745-1912) for NICU bed • Convert STAT EEG to Long term video EEG monitoring • Call Epilepsy attending and Neurocritical Care team for further management recommendations and discussion • Options: • Valproic acid: Load IV 40mg/kg; Maintenance: 30 mg/kg/day divided every 8 hours (max dose 60 mg/kg/day) Avoid use with liver failure • Lacosamide 400mg bolus ; Maintenance dose: 200 mg IV q 12 hours (max dose 600 mg daily) Monitor for PR prolongation • Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg loading dose; Maintenance dose: 1000 mg IV every 12 hours (max dose 12,000 mg daily) • Phenobarbital total loading dose of 20 mg/kg, divided into smaller 5 mg/kg loading doses at a rate of 10 mg per minute, repeated every two hours until total load given (can be given in non-intubated patient but not preferred, reduced risk of hypotension/ respiratory suppression, typical rate for convulsive status in intubated patient is 60 mg per minute) Monitor for respiratory depression, hypotension • If nonconvulsive seizures continue, intubate and load with midazolam 0.2 mg/kg IV bolus, may repeat bolus dose after 5 minutes if seizures not controlled. Start maintenance dose at 0.1 mg/kg/hr, may increase by 0.1 mg/kg/hr every 20-30 minutes until seizures controlled (usual dose 0.2-0.4 mg/kg/hr; max dose 2.9 mg/kg/hr) monitor for hypotension (patient must be intubated) • If still having nonconvulsive seizures: consider pentobarbital coma (MUST be ordered by CCM) in consultation with Epilepsy attending and General Neurology attending with further management to be determined on a case by case basis Important Practice Points  Make sure ED weighs patient on arrival; this protocol is contingent on WEIGHT BASED DOSING.  Make sure that timing of doses, infusion rate and actual doses are accurate prior to order (see EPIC status epilepticus order set once available)  If fosphenytoin is not available, can use equivalent dose of phenytoin

title: Optic Neuritis and Transverse Myelitis
text: OPTIC NEURITIS Epi: usually 20s-40s, 2:1 F:M Presentation: monocular vison loss/scotoma, red desat, painful eye movements (90%), Uhtoff’s Exam: swollen disks in 1/3 only, central vision loss, poor color plates/red hatpin, +APD Workup + Admission + Treatment: 1) Blood tests, CXR, and LP are not helpful (unless atypical ON); order MRI for prediction of MS 2) Consider formal VF testing (Goldmann or Humphrey). Consider NMO testing since treatment of this condition is different from routine optic neuritis. 3) Admit for IV Solumedrol 1g/d x 3-5 days, expect recovery in 2-3 weeks 4) Plan to start immunomodulatory therapy if MRI with >2 lesions (>3mm diam, perivent or ovoid) to reduce risk of MS: Interferon beta-1a (Avonex) group had 44% less risk of CDMS at 3 years (CHAMPS trial, Jacobs et al, 2000); interferon beta-1a (Rebif) group had 34% probability of conversion to MS at 2 years (compared to 45% of placebo; ETOMs trial, Comi et al, 2001); interferon beta-1b group had 28% probability of conversion to MS at 2 years (compared to 45% of placebo; BENEFIT trial, Kappos et al, 2006) Evidence for treatment in acute optic neuritis (Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial, 1992,1993,1997)  IV steroids - speed vision recovery, no change in acuity vs. placebo at 5 yrs  IV steroids - 50% less develop CDMS at 2 yrs (8% vs 17%), but no difference at 5 years  Oral steroids - increased recurrent ON vs IV/placebo (41% vs 25%) over 5 yrs (don’t treat acute ON with oral steroids) ACUTE TRANSVERSE MYELITIS 1. Demyelinating: NMOSD: Dx Criteria: Core Clinical Characteristics: Optic neuritis, acute myelitis, Area postrema syndrome, Acute Brainstem syndrome, Acute diencephalic syndrome Additional MRI requirements: for ON: Optic Nerve MRI inflammation >1/2 optic nerve. For myelitis: cord lesion > 3 segments, MRI brain not c/w MS, NMO-IgG positive (sen 58-76%, sp 85-99%) NMOSD with AQP4-IgG: At least 1 core clinical; Positive AQP4-IgG NMOSD with negative AQP-IgG: At least 2 core clinical (at least 1 has to be ON or TM); Dissemination in space; MRI requirements as applicable * Distinguish from MS: long cord lesions, usually nl MRI brain (atypical when they occur), CSF can have cells (PMN), neg CSF OCBs, NMO Abs rare in MS Treatment: Pheresis (acute); Rituximab (prophylaxis) at 375mg/m2 weekly x 4 weeks (Wingerchuk, et al Neurology 2015:85:1-13) 2. Infectious: Dx Testing should include HSV-1,2; EBV, VZV, lyme, HTLV-1, HHV-6, CMV, WNV 3. Misc inflammatory: SLE, Sjogren’s, MCTD, Bechet’s, Sarcoidosis 4. Nutritional: B12, folate, copper, vitamin E Approach: 1. MRI GAD  R/o Compression (if compression, call a surgeon) 2. If > 3 segments, consider NMO, SLE, Cu def, sarcoid, infarcts, infection * If pleocytosis (> 20-50 cells), check HSV, VZV, Lyme, EBV, HHV-6, HTLV-1, Enterovirus, WNV * Serologic testing should include: ANA, SSA/B, Lyme, Anti-cardiolipin Ab & Anti-phopholipid Abs, HTLV-1, HIV-1, RPR, B12, folate, Copper * More ancillary testing: Chest CT (sarcoid), Lip Biopsy (Sjogrens), Brain/Meningeal Biopsy CSF inflammation and its mimics = inflammogram  CSF: oligoclonal bands, myelin basic protein, VDRL, JC virus PCR (if immunosuppressed)  Serum: SPEP, ANA, ANCA, SSA/B, ACE, RPR, Lyme ab, HIV, B12, ESR, TSH

title: Seizure Consult
text: SEIZURE CONSULT HISTORY: New onset seizure or known epilepsy? If known epilepsy: goal is to determine AED regimen and compliance, seizure type/frequency and last seizure, epilepsy physician, any recent triggers (see below). Check AED levels and if low or they are non-compliant, re- load; if not, consider increasing AED dose. Ask about prior AEDs tried. If new onset seizures, obtain comprehensive first seizure history:  RISK FACTORS: history of febrile seizures, family history of seizures, prenatal and perinatal complications, developmental delay, history of head trauma, history of CNS infection, history of CNS mass/malformation, metabolic disorder, stroke (hemorrhagic>ischemic), drug/alcohol use, consanguinity  TRIGGERS LOWERING SEIZURE THRESHOLD: Medications, AED noncompliance, infection, metabolic abnormalities, toxic ingestion, menses, stress, sleep deprivation, EtOH/benzo withdrawal, fever, use of stimulants, hypoglycemia, electrolyte disturbance, hypoxia, acute medical/neuro illness  CHARACTERISTICS: aura (visual, auditory, olfactory, epigastric rising sensation, psychic (déjà vu, derealization, depersonalization, fear, anxiety), type of seizure (especially how its starts), eyes open or closed, lateral eye or head deviation, length of seizure, associated tongue bite or other injury, incontinence, post-ictal confusion, post-ictal weakness.  SUBTLE SEIZURE SIGNS: history of staring, automatisms, waking up incontinent or injured (tongue bite), myoclonic jerks. EXAM: Focus on mental status, post-ictal paralysis or other focal deficits, subtle signs of ongoing seizures such as abnormal movements, head or eye deviation, eyelid twitching, pupillary abnormalities or hippus; tongue bite/other injury, and meningeal signs ED WORKUP: AED levels (only carbamazepine, phenytoin, valproate and phenobarb levels are available acutely, but others can be helpful to establish compliance), non-contrast head CT (new seizures only!), can consider MRI with/without contrast (seizure protocol). Basic labs including electrolytes and CBC, infectious workup (UA, CXR), toxicology screen, LP if concern for meningitis PLAN:  AED: load and then start maintenance dose (do not start AEDs for an uncomplicated first seizure)  EEG: highest yield within 24-48 h. If the patient is not “back to baseline,” consider stat EEG.  MRI with and without contrast, seizure protocol if warranted  Ativan IV PRN seizure activity  Evaluate medication list for medications that lower the seizure threshold  Can be discharged from the ED if they only had 1-2 seizure, have returned to baseline, and have nothing in the above workup requiring admission.  Information patient that he/she cannot drive for ≥ 6 months per Florida state law  Refer to the Urgent Clinic follow-up or your continuity clinic via Epic. Not Returning to Baseline after a Seizure The two most common reasons patients don’t wake up are prolonged post-ictal periods and overly eager benzo administration … But don’t miss anything important:  Make sure the patient isn’t still seizing. Look for subtle abnormal limb or eye movement, fluctuating arousal or complete lack of improvement. 40% of patients with status epilepticus continue having subclinical seizures after convulsive seizures are controlled. Consider a stat EEG.  Get a head CT if you don’t have one yet  Consider LP if you have any concern for meningitis/encephalitis or SAH not seen on HCT  You may need to admit a patient overnight for prolonged postical period or benzo intoxication. If there is no medical reason they had a seizure (e.g. EtOH w/d, infection) they will likely need to be admitted to neurology.

title: Stroke Admission Orders
text: Possible complications of IV tPA: intracranial hemorrhage and angioedema Angioedema: Immediately assess for airway compromise, give 0.5 ml IM Epinephrine and 25 mg IV Benadryl Intracranial hemorrhage: If the patient’s clinical status deteriorates after administration of IV tPA, suspect an intracranial bleed. CALL THE ATTENDING OR FELLOW IMMEDIATELY. After administration of IV t-PA, monitor the patient for the following symptoms:  Worsening level of consciousness (by GCS) or new/worsening neurological symptoms  Nausea, vomiting, and/or sudden, severe headache  Elevated blood pressure and/or bradycardia (the Cushing response, signs of increased ICP)  Seizure If intracranial hemorrhage is suspected:  Obtain CT head stat  Do not leave the patient’s bedside until he/she is stable If CT head shows an intracranial hemorrhage - DISCUSS IMMEDIATELY WITH STROKE ATTENDING:  Obtain type & screen  Tighten BP control, goal SBP 100-140, consider nicardipine infusion  Check STAT fibrinogen and platelets  If fibrinogen is <200 mg/dl, give 0.1 u/kg cryoprecipitate  Consider urgent Neurosurgery consult  If bleeding is refractory and/or life-threatening, consider aminocaproic acid 1 g/10 kg IV Stroke Admission Orders and Workup: exceptions to guidelines if tPA given  For routine stroke admissions, use order set “NEU STROKE PROGRAM ISCHEMIC STROKE ADMISSION” Post-tPA or mechanical thrombectomy patients use order set “NEU STROKE PROGRAM ORDERS – POST ALTEPLASE (T-PA) ADMISSION ORDERS IP UF”  Bedside dysphagia screen performed by nursing, must be documented in chart. If the patient fails, he/she MUST have formal swallow evaluation.  Patient who fail dysphagia screen must be strict NPO (this includes oral medications!) until they are cleared by speech/swallow  Make sure any medication orders accurately reflect the patient’s po status (eg oral or DHT route)  ASA 325 mg PO if swallowing, 300 mg PR if not (unless on anticoagulation or tPA in last 24 hours)  Statin  NS @ 100 cc/hr (careful with patients with CHF or on HD). Avoid hypotonic fluids.  acetaminophen 650 mg q4h prn temp >100.4  sliding scale insulin and fingersticks q6h  neurochecks q4h on floor (q2h in IMC, q1h in NICU)  UA/UDS  CXR  EKG in ED, telemetry/monitoring after admission  SCD (all) plus Heparin SQ 5,000 units q8h (unless tPA in last 24 hours)  Permissive hypertension (SBP<220, DBP<110), hold home BP meds (except ½ dose β-blocker) o Exception: BP goal will be lower following IV tPA (<180/105) or mechanical thrombectomy (goal depends on procedure result, discuss with fellow/attending)  Labs: lipid panel, HgbA1c, RPR, liver function tests, troponin, TSH, folate/B12  If concerned for endocarditis, send serial blood cultures  Transthoracic echocardiogram (+/- bubble study if <60 years old)  MRI brain  MRA head and neck vs. carotid ultrasound/transcranial dopplers if patient has not had CTA head/neck **Certain patients (e.g., younger patients) may require TEE, hypercoagulable or vasculitis workup. Discuss with attending before ordering. Hypercoagulable panel: Antiphospholipid panel (anticardiolipin, DRVVT, beta-2 microglobulin, prothrombin gene mutation, Factor V Leiden mutation, Protein C activity, Protein S activity, antithrombin III activity, HIT antibody (in appropriate clinical setting).

title: Stroke Alert
text:
STROKE ALERT ALL ALERTS MUST BE DISCUSSED WITH STROKE ATTENDING/FELLOW IN REAL TIME Joint Commission goals:  Door to neuro eval: <15 min  Door to CT completion: <20 min  CT complete to interpretation: <20 min (use the time stamp in Visage if you interpreted in real time)  Door to IV tPA: <30 min  Door to groin puncture (thrombectomy): <60 min Time guidelines: Intravenous tPA: < 4.5 hours of symptom onset Mechanical thrombectomy: < 6h in most cases, potentially up to 24h depending on perfusion scan Initial steps: 1. When was the patient last known normal? Don’t trust anybody - FIND OUT YOURSELF. Obtain family phone contact information from EMS in case needed for timeline confirmation and eventual consent. 2. BRIEF medical history (similar sx in past, acuity of onset, fluctuation/progression of sx, known stroke causes [Afib, carotid disease], associated sx (headache/neck pain, altered consciousness, abnormal movements), current antithrombotics 3. Check blood pressure 4. Fingerstick glucose. Correct if <50 or >400. If patient is still symptomatic, proceed with acute vascular workup 5. Rapid neuro exam/NIHSS – do NOT delay CT/IV/blood draw. Learn to work around ED providers. 6. Take patient to CT scan as soon as possible.  UF stroke alert protocol is non-contrast HCT, CTA head/neck, and CT perfusion  Non-contrast HCT alone is sufficient for tPA decision. If the perfusion scanner is down, do NOT waste time taking the patient to a different tower.  Stay with the patient and immediately review the scan yourself. Do NOT wait for CTA to begin to mobilize for IV tPA. CTA/CTP requires post-processing and is NOT necessary for IV tPA. 7. Review tPA inclusion/exclusion criteria to confirm patient is eligible 8. Call stroke attending/fellow as soon as possible to discuss treatment plan. 9. Review risks/benefits with patient and/or family. Obtain verbal consent if possible. See below for suggested language. If patient cannot provide consent and family is not immediately available by phone, two attendings (stroke and ED) can provide emergency consent. Do not waste time making more than 1-2 attempts to contact family. 10. If IV tPA is planned:  Tenectaplase for all patients  Inform nurse/pharmacist/ED team of plan  Get weight in kilograms (if not obtained on arrival)  Order tPA in Epic. TNK (single bolus): 0.25 mg/kg; max dose 25 mg  Ensure patient has 2 well-functioning IVs, preferably 20-gauge or larger  Make sure BP is <185/110 before pushing tPA bolus**  Remain with the patient at bedside until tPA is administered to ensure there is no delay. 11. Consider patient for mechanical thrombectomy (see below) and/or acute stroke clinical trial. *Suggested language for consent: “You are having an acute stroke, caused by a blood clot blocking one of the arteries in your brain. The symptoms you’re currently having may become permanent if we don’t act quickly. You are eligible for a medication called tPA, which will help dissolve the blood clot. Studies in thousands of patients with strokes like yours have shown that with this medication, patients have a much better chance of recovering to normal and living at home after a stroke. Because this medication temporarily thins your blood, there is a possible risk of bleeding, including bleeding in the brain. However, the potential benefit from the treatment is 6–10 times greater than the risks of harm, and I believe this medication is the best chance we have of reversing your symptoms. The faster we start the treatment, the better your chances of recovery. Do we have your permission to proceed?” **If BP elevated: 1) Labetalol 10-20mg IV over 1-2 minutes, may repeat x 1 OR 2) Start Nicardipine gtt at 5mg/hr IV, titrate up by 2.5mg/hr at 5-15 minute intervals, max dose: 15mg/hr. Can consider clevidipine gtt. - If BP does not improve despite these interventions, then discuss eligibility further with attending/fellow.
IV tPA Inclusion Criteria:  Clinical diagnosis of stroke with neurological deficit that would be considered disabling  Established time of symptom onset/last seen normal within past 4.5 hours Potential tPA exclusions to consider: (Note: many of these rules are flexible in the correct clinical scenario, therefore each case MUST be discussed expeditiously with stroke attending/fellow)  Significant head trauma or prior stroke in previous 3 months  Symptoms suggestive of subarachnoid hemorrhage  Arterial puncture at noncompressible site in previous 7 days  History of previous intracranial hemorrhage 1. Intracranial neoplasm, aneurysm, or arteriovenous malformation 2. Recent intracranial or intraspinal surgery 3. SBP > 185, DBP > 110 at time of treatment despite appropriate therapy 4. Active internal bleeding 5. Platelets <100K 6. Heparin received within 48 hours with elevated PTT > 40 sec 7. Current use of anticoagulant with INR > 1.7 or PT > 15 sec 8. Current use of direct thrombin inhibitors or factor Xa inhibitors 9. CT demonstrates large lobar infarction (hypodensity > 1/3 cerebral hemisphere) 10. Blood glucose concentration < 50mg/dL (correct first but treat if concern for stroke) 11. Minor or rapidly improving stroke symptoms (ensure residual is not disabling) 12. Pregnancy 13. Seizure at onset with postictal residual neurological impairments 14. Major surgery or serious trauma within previous 14 days 15. Recent GI or GU tract hemorrhage within previous 21 days 16. Acute MI within previous 3 months * In patients without history of thrombocytopenia, IV tPA can be initiated before availability of platelet count, but should be discontinued if plt < 100k ** In patients without recent use of oral anticoagulants or heparin, treatment with tPA can be initiated before availability of coagulation test results but should be discontinued if INR > 1. 7. *** Caution if recent hemodialysis with administration of heparin bolus Mechanical thrombectomy Consideration for mechanical thrombectomy should begin immediately after tPA decision is made. Eligibility varies depending on time from symptom onset/last known well 1) Symptom onset <6h: requires large-vessel occlusion on CTA (or suspected based on clinical exam), NIHSS ≥ 6, ASPECTS (determined from non-contrast HCT) ≥ 6. Do NOT wait for CTP results per AHA guidelines. 2) Symptom onset 6-24 hours: large-vessel occlusion on CTA, CTP suggestive of large penumbra/small core meeting criteria from DAWN/DEFUSE-3 trials. Hyperacute-MRI protocol can be considered in select patients who are unable to tolerate contrast (discuss with attending). Review expeditiously with the stroke fellow/attending. If the patient is appropriate for intervention, the stroke attending will call the endovascular neurosurgery attending directly. - Call ShandsCair (#50300 on house phone) or #69 (inpatient stroke alerts) to request a STROKE ENDOVASCULAR THERAPY ALERT. Dispatch will page out the following stroke alert: “[Patient room number/MRN] confirmed for endovascular therapy” - Page neurosurgery consult resident (413-4736 or 413-2125) to inform that patient has been accepted for intervention by endovascular. Also call the IR tech to notify of an incoming case (352-256-5402). -Accompany the patient to the OR to provide in-person signout to the anesthesia team. -Place admission orders ASAP (see below) so that the patient can leave the OR expeditiously -Accompany the patient to the NICU for signout and to perform/document a post-procedure NIHSS. Orders after IV tPA treatment and/or mechanical thrombectomy (use order set “NEU STROKE PROGRAM ORDERS - POST ALTEPLASE (T-PA) ADMISSION ORDERS IP UF”):  Admit to NICU (call CCM fellow to notify)  Monitor BP (maintain <180/105 post tPA, discuss goal BP with attending for mechanical thrombectomy): vitals q15 min x 2 h after start, then q30 min x 6 h, then q1 h x 16 h  No heparin, warfarin, aspirin, other antiplatelets/anticoagulation for 24 hours if pt received tPA  Avoid invasive procedures (eg, no NG tubes, no Foley) and arterial access at non-compressible sites for 24 h if patient received tPA  Repeat CT scan 24 hours post tPA to exclude hemorrhage – if negative can give ASA 325 mg, start DVT prophylaxis

title: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
text: NEUROCRITICAL CARE Intracranial Vascular Malformations Vascular Malformation Rupture Risk and Suggested Treatment Type Yearly Risk Factors Treatment rupture risk AVM 5% Size (>3 cm), deep venous Surgical, gamma knife embolization, drainage, nidal aneurysms, observation depending on location, size, prior hemorrhage age Cavernous 2-3% Prior hemorrhage Surgical vs observation malformation Venous angioma 0.1-0.5% Large size Not warranted Capillary Unknown – Not warranted telangiectasia (rare) Subarachnoid hemorrhage Thunderclap SAH, pituitary apoplexy, arterial dissection, venous sinus thrombosis, hypertensive headache ddx urgency, RCVS, intracranial hypotension, primary thunderclap headache Sulcal subarachnoid RCVS, venous sinus thrombosis, vasculitis, mycotic aneurysm, trauma, amyloid hemorrhage ddx angiopathy, meningeal neoplasm, dural AV fistula, meningitis Immediate Management in the ED To conform the diagnosis:  Non-contrast CT  LP if CT findings are normal (xanthochromia might be falsely negative if <6h from symptom onset) Follow up Management after confirmed SAH  Obtain CBC, electrolytes, Ca, Mg, PT/PTT, glucose  ECG and CXR to rule out neurogenic pulmonary edema or cardiac stunning  Keep SBP <160 mm Hg until aneurysm secured. Use labetalol 10 mg IV if HR >60, hydralazine 10 mg IV for HR <60  Neurosurgery consultation for angiography and/or EVD  EVD if patient has depressed LOC and hydrocephalus  Tranexamic acid 1 g IV if onset within past 72 hours; caution in patients with severe coronary artery  disease or who have a delayed presentation and may already be in vasospasm  Monitor for complications: rebleeding, hydrocephalus, vasospasm and hyponatremia due to cerebral salt wasting or SIADH  Call NICU consultant, admit to ICU  Bedside dysphagia evaluation to be performed by admitting resident. NPO if fails o Consider formal SLP assessment if facial weakness/significant dysarthria o No oral meds prior to bedside dysphagia screen Admission Orders 1. Include WFNS, Modified Fisher, Hunt and Hess scores in initial note (See tables below) 2. Admit to NICU unit 47 under neurosurgery service 3. Neuro checks per unit routine 4. Order STAT head CT if neurologic deterioration. 5. Blood pressure management: Labetalol 10 mg, every 15 minutes if HR >60, alternatively use hydralazine 10 mg for HR <60. For refractory HTN, initiate nicardipine infusion and titrate to effect. a. Unsecured aneurysm, keep SBP <160 mm Hg b. Consider inserting arterial line for dynamic monitoring 6. Continue TXA 1g q6h until aneurysm is secured (no more than 72h); caution in pts with severe cardiac disease 7. Nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours by mouth or NG tube for 21 days for delayed cerebral ischemia prophylaxis. Transition to 30 mg q2h if patient develops hypotension with typical dosing. 8. Monitor for elevated intracranial pressure 9. Discuss management with NCCM fellow and neurosurgery 10. Strict bedrest and strict I/Os, insert urinary catheter 11. Daily electrolytes to monitor for hyponatremia

title: TBI
text: Traumatic Brain Injury All patients with: 1. Risk of intracranial hypertension (ICP = 22 mmHg) or 2. GCS = 9 and abnormal CT scan or 3. GCS = 9 with normal CT scan and 2 of the following a. Systolic blood pressure < 90 mmHg b. Age > 40 c. Decorticate or Decerebrate posturing Must have the following on admission to the ICU: 1. ICP monitor to be placed within 6 hours a. EVD or intraparenchymal 2. Cerebral Oximetry Monitor to be placed within 6 hours a. Parenchymal 3. Invasive arterial monitoring 4. CO monitoring a. Bedside TTE/hTTE or Flotrack 5. Central venous line and CVP monitoring 6. Bladder temperature 7. ETCO2 Monitor 8. 24 hour EEG within 24 hours of admission 9. Keppra IV infusion a. 500 mg bid x 1 week Goals of Management: 1. Maintain CPP = 60 (CPP = MAP – ICP) 2. Core Temperature = 37.5 degrees Celsius 3. ICP = 22 mmHg 4. PbtO2 (cerebral oxygen) = 20 mmHg 5. Target PaCO2 of 35-40 mmHg 6. Sodium goal of 145-150 mmol/L 7. Maintain SpO2 > 90% and PaO2 > 80 mmHg 8. Hemoglobin > 7 g/dL 9. Blood sugar per ICU protocol 100-180mg/dL 10. Ensure adequate sedation to maintain ICP goals 11. Management of elevated ICP and decreased PbtO2 using UF severe TBI protocol Paroxysmal Sympathetic Hyperactivity (PSH) PSH is a disorder in the regulation of autonomic function with acute brain injury, most notably severe TBI  Recurrent episodes of excessive sympathetic activity that start rapidly, are often induced by stimulation, and resolve spontaneously after a variable period of time or after administration of an abortive medication  Core clinical features include tachycardia, hypertension, tachypnea, hyperthermia, sweating, and/or increased muscle tone with possible dystonic posturing  Associated conditions —brain trauma, nontraumatic forms of acute brain disease, including anoxic-ischemic coma after cardiac arrest, intracranial hemorrhage, autoimmune encephalitis, ischemic stroke, hydrocephalus, and cerebral fat embolism syndrome

title: TIA Risk Stratification and Stroke Mechanisms
text: TIAs: As a general rule of thumb, all TIA patients should be observed for 24 hours and evaluated expeditiously for high- risk stroke mechanism. If they want to go home, tell them the risk: The ABCD2 score predicts risk of stroke within 2 days of a TIA (Lancet 369: 282-292, 2007): Age ≥60 years [1 point] Blood pressure ≥140/90 mm Hg [1 point] Clinical features: unilateral weakness [2 points], speech impairment without weakness [1 point] Duration ≥60 min [2 points] or 10–59 min [1 point] Diabetes [1 point] Score 6–7 = High risk = 8.1% 2-day risk 4–5 = Moderate risk = 4.1% 0–3 = Low risk = 1.0% Stroke mechanisms: Most common mechanisms of stroke include:  Cardioembolism (~20% ischemic stroke): Atrial fibrillation (most common, esp in older adults), CHF/CM with low EF, LV or LAA thrombus, cardiac tumor (myxoma/fibroelastoma), mechanical valve, endocarditis (infectious or marantic [autoimmune/malignancy]), paradoxical embolism (PFO, pulm AVM)  Large-artery atherosclerosis (~15%) – extracranial or intracranial (affects treatment choice)  Small-vessel disease (~25%) – must be subcortical/brainstem/cerebellum. Small cortical strokes are generally embolic. Lacunar strokes are usually linked to vascular risk factors, esp uncontrolled DM and tobacco  Rare causes (<5%) – see below, also consider hypercoagulable state from undiagnosed malignancy  Cryptogenic stroke (~20-30%) – no cause found despite thorough workup. If embolic-appearing, may be ESUS (embolic stroke of undetermined source), consider long-term Afib monitoring, no role for empiric AC per ESUS trials to date. Young Stroke: In addition to conventional stroke mechanisms, consider the following for patients <60 years old: Cardiac disease (including congenital, rheumatic valve disease, mitral valve prolapse, patent foramen ovale, endocarditis, atrial myxoma, arrhythmias, cardiac surgery) Large vessel disease  Premature atherosclerosis – consider radiation vasculopathy in patients with previous head/neck irradiation. Also consider HIV  Dissection (spontaneous or traumatic)  Inherited metabolic diseases (homocystinuria, Fabry’s, pseudoxanthoma elasticum, MELAS syndrome)  Fibromuscular dysplasia  Infection (bacterial, fungal, tuberculosis, syphilis, Lyme)  Vasculitis (collagen vascular diseases — systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren’s syndrome, polyarteritis nodosa; Takayasu’s disease, Wegener’s syndrome, cryoglobulinemia, sarcoidosis, inflammatory bowel disease, isolated central nervous system angiitis)  Moyamoya disease  Toxic (illicit drugs — cocaine, heroin, phencyclidine; therapeutic drugs — L-asparaginase, cytosine arabinoside) Small vessel disease  Vasculopathy (infectious, noninfectious, microangiopathy) Hematologic disease  Sickle-cell disease, leukemia, thrombocytosis, polycythemia vera  Hypercoagulable states (antiphospholipid antibody syndromes, deficiency of antithrombin III or protein S or C, resistance to activated protein C, increased factor VIII, MTHFR)  DIC, TTP  Venous occlusion (dehydration, parameningeal infection, meningitis, neoplasm, polycythemia, leukemia, inflammatory bowel disease)

title: Vascular Neurology
text:
STROKE ALERT ALL ALERTS MUST BE DISCUSSED WITH STROKE ATTENDING/FELLOW IN REAL TIME Joint Commission goals:  Door to neuro eval: <15 min  Door to CT completion: <20 min  CT complete to interpretation: <20 min (use the time stamp in Visage if you interpreted in real time)  Door to IV tPA: <30 min  Door to groin puncture (thrombectomy): <60 min Time guidelines: Intravenous tPA: < 4.5 hours of symptom onset Mechanical thrombectomy: < 6h in most cases, potentially up to 24h depending on perfusion scan Initial steps: 1. When was the patient last known normal? Don’t trust anybody - FIND OUT YOURSELF. Obtain family phone contact information from EMS in case needed for timeline confirmation and eventual consent. 2. BRIEF medical history (similar sx in past, acuity of onset, fluctuation/progression of sx, known stroke causes [Afib, carotid disease], associated sx (headache/neck pain, altered consciousness, abnormal movements), current antithrombotics 3. Check blood pressure 4. Fingerstick glucose. Correct if <50 or >400. If patient is still symptomatic, proceed with acute vascular workup 5. Rapid neuro exam/NIHSS – do NOT delay CT/IV/blood draw. Learn to work around ED providers. 6. Take patient to CT scan as soon as possible.  UF stroke alert protocol is non-contrast HCT, CTA head/neck, and CT perfusion  Non-contrast HCT alone is sufficient for tPA decision. If the perfusion scanner is down, do NOT waste time taking the patient to a different tower.  Stay with the patient and immediately review the scan yourself. Do NOT wait for CTA to begin to mobilize for IV tPA. CTA/CTP requires post-processing and is NOT necessary for IV tPA. 7. Review tPA inclusion/exclusion criteria to confirm patient is eligible 8. Call stroke attending/fellow as soon as possible to discuss treatment plan. 9. Review risks/benefits with patient and/or family. Obtain verbal consent if possible. See below for suggested language. If patient cannot provide consent and family is not immediately available by phone, two attendings (stroke and ED) can provide emergency consent. Do not waste time making more than 1-2 attempts to contact family. 10. If IV tPA is planned:  Tenectaplase for all patients  Inform nurse/pharmacist/ED team of plan  Get weight in kilograms (if not obtained on arrival)  Order tPA in Epic. TNK (single bolus): 0.25 mg/kg; max dose 25 mg  Ensure patient has 2 well-functioning IVs, preferably 20-gauge or larger  Make sure BP is <185/110 before pushing tPA bolus**  Remain with the patient at bedside until tPA is administered to ensure there is no delay. 11. Consider patient for mechanical thrombectomy (see below) and/or acute stroke clinical trial. *Suggested language for consent: “You are having an acute stroke, caused by a blood clot blocking one of the arteries in your brain. The symptoms you’re currently having may become permanent if we don’t act quickly. You are eligible for a medication called tPA, which will help dissolve the blood clot. Studies in thousands of patients with strokes like yours have shown that with this medication, patients have a much better chance of recovering to normal and living at home after a stroke. Because this medication temporarily thins your blood, there is a possible risk of bleeding, including bleeding in the brain. However, the potential benefit from the treatment is 6–10 times greater than the risks of harm, and I believe this medication is the best chance we have of reversing your symptoms. The faster we start the treatment, the better your chances of recovery. Do we have your permission to proceed?” **If BP elevated: 1) Labetalol 10-20mg IV over 1-2 minutes, may repeat x 1 OR 2) Start Nicardipine gtt at 5mg/hr IV, titrate up by 2.5mg/hr at 5-15 minute intervals, max dose: 15mg/hr. Can consider clevidipine gtt. - If BP does not improve despite these interventions, then discuss eligibility further with attending/fellow.
IV tPA Inclusion Criteria:  Clinical diagnosis of stroke with neurological deficit that would be considered disabling  Established time of symptom onset/last seen normal within past 4.5 hours Potential tPA exclusions to consider: (Note: many of these rules are flexible in the correct clinical scenario, therefore each case MUST be discussed expeditiously with stroke attending/fellow)  Significant head trauma or prior stroke in previous 3 months  Symptoms suggestive of subarachnoid hemorrhage  Arterial puncture at noncompressible site in previous 7 days  History of previous intracranial hemorrhage 1. Intracranial neoplasm, aneurysm, or arteriovenous malformation 2. Recent intracranial or intraspinal surgery 3. SBP > 185, DBP > 110 at time of treatment despite appropriate therapy 4. Active internal bleeding 5. Platelets <100K 6. Heparin received within 48 hours with elevated PTT > 40 sec 7. Current use of anticoagulant with INR > 1.7 or PT > 15 sec 8. Current use of direct thrombin inhibitors or factor Xa inhibitors 9. CT demonstrates large lobar infarction (hypodensity > 1/3 cerebral hemisphere) 10. Blood glucose concentration < 50mg/dL (correct first but treat if concern for stroke) 11. Minor or rapidly improving stroke symptoms (ensure residual is not disabling) 12. Pregnancy 13. Seizure at onset with postictal residual neurological impairments 14. Major surgery or serious trauma within previous 14 days 15. Recent GI or GU tract hemorrhage within previous 21 days 16. Acute MI within previous 3 months * In patients without history of thrombocytopenia, IV tPA can be initiated before availability of platelet count, but should be discontinued if plt < 100k ** In patients without recent use of oral anticoagulants or heparin, treatment with tPA can be initiated before availability of coagulation test results but should be discontinued if INR > 1. 7. *** Caution if recent hemodialysis with administration of heparin bolus Mechanical thrombectomy Consideration for mechanical thrombectomy should begin immediately after tPA decision is made. Eligibility varies depending on time from symptom onset/last known well 1) Symptom onset <6h: requires large-vessel occlusion on CTA (or suspected based on clinical exam), NIHSS ≥ 6, ASPECTS (determined from non-contrast HCT) ≥ 6. Do NOT wait for CTP results per AHA guidelines. 2) Symptom onset 6-24 hours: large-vessel occlusion on CTA, CTP suggestive of large penumbra/small core meeting criteria from DAWN/DEFUSE-3 trials. Hyperacute-MRI protocol can be considered in select patients who are unable to tolerate contrast (discuss with attending). Review expeditiously with the stroke fellow/attending. If the patient is appropriate for intervention, the stroke attending will call the endovascular neurosurgery attending directly. - Call ShandsCair (#50300 on house phone) or #69 (inpatient stroke alerts) to request a STROKE ENDOVASCULAR THERAPY ALERT. Dispatch will page out the following stroke alert: “[Patient room number/MRN] confirmed for endovascular therapy” - Page neurosurgery consult resident (413-4736 or 413-2125) to inform that patient has been accepted for intervention by endovascular. Also call the IR tech to notify of an incoming case (352-256-5402). -Accompany the patient to the OR to provide in-person signout to the anesthesia team. -Place admission orders ASAP (see below) so that the patient can leave the OR expeditiously -Accompany the patient to the NICU for signout and to perform/document a post-procedure NIHSS. Orders after IV tPA treatment and/or mechanical thrombectomy (use order set “NEU STROKE PROGRAM ORDERS - POST ALTEPLASE (T-PA) ADMISSION ORDERS IP UF”):  Admit to NICU (call CCM fellow to notify)  Monitor BP (maintain <180/105 post tPA, discuss goal BP with attending for mechanical thrombectomy): vitals q15 min x 2 h after start, then q30 min x 6 h, then q1 h x 16 h  No heparin, warfarin, aspirin, other antiplatelets/anticoagulation for 24 hours if pt received tPA  Avoid invasive procedures (eg, no NG tubes, no Foley) and arterial access at non-compressible sites for 24 h if patient received tPA  Repeat CT scan 24 hours post tPA to exclude hemorrhage – if negative can give ASA 325 mg, start DVT prophylaxis

title: VA Stroke Alert
text: VA STROKE ALERTS with concern for large vessel occlusion Evaluate patient emergently and obtain noncontrast head CT as you would normally. Do not give contrast (i.e. CTA) at VA if concern for large vessel occlusion, as availability at the VA will introduce a time delay. You can give tPA at the VA after discussion with attending if indicated. If concern for large vessel stroke that may be amenable to endovascular intervention, bring this to the attention of VA attending immediately. If the VA attending agrees to transfer to UF, he/she must call the Shands transfer center (352-265-0559) to speak with the on-call stroke attending - do NOT bypass this step by calling the stroke attending directly. Once the patient has been accepted for transfer, follow the steps below.* If patient is within tPA window do not delay tPA administration, you can give bolus and start infusion while setting up the transfer.** Make sure nurse continues to monitor blood pressure with tPA infusion. To transfer to UF: Regular Hours (8-4:30p, M-F) 1. Call VA transfer coordinator (Teresa Taylor @ 352-264-3848 or 352-339-9126 or Sharonda Taylor- Martin @ 386-487-1750 ext 1788) and inform them of need for emergent transfer 2. Place a Non-VA Care (NVCC) Consult (CPRSConsults (order menu)Non VA Care 3. Complete BENEFICIARY TRAVEL (BT) note template (Notes section of CPRS). After completing the template, an order menu will open, select “GNV Travel IP” option and complete the travel consult order. 4. Obtain consent using iMed Consent. 5. Enter discharge order (In the Discharge Order dialogue box, next to “Discharge Patient” type in “Hospital to Hospital Transfer to Shands pending bed availability.” 6. Write a consult note/DC summary Afterhours/ weekends/holidays AOD will act as the VA Transfer Coordinator and may be reached at 78-6724 or 78-6004, but otherwise the process will be the same as during regular hours. *Make resident on service and/or vascular fellow at UF aware of transfer as well. **Stay with patient until ambulance arrives to transfer to UF. Lake City VA telephone requests Treatment recommendations can only be given for patients whom you have personally evaluated. If you receive a request for telephone consultation from the Lake City VA, the following are the only two available options:  If the description sounds like a potential acute stroke within a therapeutic treatment window, you should advise the physician to contact the VA National Tele-Stroke program from their location or immediately transfer the patient to the nearest Comprehensive Stroke Center of their choice.  If the physician calling you describes ANYTHING else, please state the following verbatim: “As a resident in training, I am not permitted by my Service Chief and Program Directors to provide consultation over the telephone. If your patient needs urgent Neurological Consultation, I will be happy to evaluate the patient if he or she is transferred to this hospital or ED. If your patient does not need urgent Neurological Consultation, please submit a Consult or E-Consult request to LKC or GNV Neurology.”
